culver-city.10202 Washington Blvd Culver City, CA 90:
The History: Originally founded in 1915 as Triangle Studios, this massive lot was taken over by Louis B. Mayer and eventually became the legendary Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer (MGM) studios. It was the birthplace of cinematic masterpieces like The Wizard of Oz and Singin' in the Rain.

The Character: Now owned by Sony, the 45-acre campus remains one of the busiest working studio lots in the world. Visitors can see the historic Colonnade building, walk beneath the soaring 94-foot steel rainbow installation, and explore the incredibly realistic facades of the "Main Street" backlot where countless television shows and commercials are filmed.
culver-city.10352 Venice Boulevard: The home of Campos Famous Burritos, a long-standing neighborhood staple known for its authentic Mexican recipes.
culver-city.10434 Culver Blvd, Culver City, CA 90232: This site is home to The Culver Steps, a modern mixed-use development that serves as a public gathering space.
culver-city.10526 Venice Blvd, Culver City CA 90232: The Rediscover Center, a community makerspace and non-profit dedicated to sustainable art education.
culver-city.11155 Washington Pl Culver City, CA: The Cozy Inn, a classic neighborhood dive bar with vintage shuffleboard and a 1950s atmosphere.
culver-city.11547 Braddock Dr. Culver City, CA: Historically known as the Doris Bither House, this residence was the real-life basis for the 1982 supernatural film The Entity.
culver-city.3520 Hayden Ave, Culver City, CA 90232: Home to the Beehive, a conceptually challenging office structure designed by architect Eric Owen Moss.
culver-city.3535 Hayden Avenue: The Beehive, an architectural landmark designed by Eric Owen Moss, featuring a distinctive staircase wrapped around four columns to create a "kinetic" appearance.
culver-city.3816 Prospect Avenue Culver City, CA: A notable location in Culver City.
culver-city.3820 Overland Avenue Culver City: A notable location in Culver City.
culver-city.3850 Sepulveda Blvd Culver City, CA 9023: The Astro Motel, a classic mid-century motel built in 1940 that remains a fixture of the Westside hospitality landscape.
culver-city.4114 Van Buren Pl: Bruce Lee once lived in this residential property located on a quiet, tree-lined street just blocks from the Sony Pictures Studios lot.
culver-city.4130 Overland Avenue, Culver City, CA 90: Mayme A. Clayton Collection of African American History & Culture - claytonmuseum.org3.
culver-city.4411 Sepulveda Boulevard, Culver City, C: Known as The Braddock, this building is a scaled-down replica of Los Angeles' Union Station and formerly housed the famous Allied Model Trains.
culver-city.5741 Buckingham Parkway: , Suite E, Culver City, CA. 90230 - Wende Museum - A Museum and Archive of the Cold War.
culver-city.6300 Hetzler Rd, Culver City, CA 90232: The gateway to the Baldwin Hills Scenic Overlook, famous for the "Culver City Stairs" which offer panoramic views of the entire LA basin.
culver-city.8641 Washington Boulevard, Culver City,: Vibrant Latin dining & dancing destination with DJs & live music, plus cocktails.
culver-city.Baldwin Hills Scenic Overlook: A hilltop park famous for the Culver City Stairs, offering 360-degree views of the LA Basin and interactive exhibits on local geology.
culver-city.Bhagavad Gita Museum: The First American Transcendental Interactive Museum, featuring high-tech diorama galleries depicting scenes from the Bhagavad Gita.
culver-city.Citizen Public Market: Food hall housed in the historic 1929 Culver City Citizen publishing building.
culver-city.Culver Hotel: Historic 1924 flatiron hotel in downtown Culver City; famous for housing the Munchkin cast during the filming of The Wizard of Oz.
culver-city.Exceptional Children's Foundation:
The History: This site serves as a major hub for the ECF, a non-profit founded in 1946 to provide services for children and adults with special needs.

The Context: The building is part of Culver City’s long history of hosting essential social services and educational centers that anchor the local residential community.
culver-city.Helms Bakery District: Historic former bakery complex converted into a design and dining destination.
culver-city.Johnnie's Pastrami: A legendary mid-century delicatessen with an iconic neon sign and authentic Googie architecture, serving local patrons since 1952.
culver-city.Kirk Douglas Theatre: Performing arts venue housed in the historic 1920s Culver Theater.
culver-city.Little Brazil: Location: Venice Boulevard (between Overland Ave and Sepulveda Blvd).\n\nStatus: Unofficial but widely recognized. This stretch is the heart of the Brazilian community in LA, anchored by the long-standing Café Brasil and several Brazilian markets and travel agencies.
culver-city.Mandrake Bar: A long-time anchor of the Culver City Arts District, this bar and gallery space is a favorite among local artists and curators.
culver-city.Robert Frost Auditorium: Striking parabolic-roofed mid-century modern auditorium on the Culver City High School campus.
culver-city.The Hobbit Houses (Dunn Drive): A collection of whimsical cottages built by a Disney artist; featuring warped rooflines and hand-carved details.
culver-city.The Hobbit Houses (Lawrence and Martha J: Tucked into a dense residential street near the Sony Pictures lot, this multi-family complex was designed and built in the 1940s by Lawrence Joseph, a former Disney artist. The whimsical cottages feature swooping shingled roofs, stained glass, and nautical themes that look like a slice of Middle-earth in the middle of the city.
culver-city.The Museum of Jurassic Technology: From the outside, it looks like a mundane storefront, but inside is a deeply surreal, dimly lit labyrinth that blends fact, fiction, and optical illusions into a modern cabinet of curiosities that defies traditional museum classification.
culver-city.The Pterodactyl: A jagged steel-and-glass 'bird' structure perched atop a parking garage; a landmark of the city's architectural radicalism.
culver-city.The SmogShoppe:
Architectural Style: Adaptive Reuse / Sustainable Design

The History: Originally a functioning smog check center built in the 1980s, this site was transformed into one of the most sustainable event spaces in the US.

Key Architectural Features: A masterclass in vertical gardening, the facility is covered in 2,000 square feet of "living walls" and utilizes vintage desert plants and salvaged materials to create a lush, urban oasis in a former industrial zone.
culver-city.Titos Tacos (Est. 1959): An iconic LA "hard shell" taco stand that has maintained a massive, loyal following and a frozen-in-time aesthetic for decades.
culver-city.Washington Building: Historic triangular flatiron-style building in downtown Culver City.

culver-city-arts-district.Row DTLA / Alameda Produce Market:
The History: Originally built between 1917 and 1923 as the Los Angeles Terminal Market, this massive complex was the primary distribution hub for the city's produce.

Modern Era: Now known as ROW DTLA, the site has undergone one of the city's largest adaptive reuse projects, transforming 30+ acres of historic industrial warehouses into a high-end creative district featuring offices, retail, and the famous Smorgasburg LA food market.

cypress-park.King Taco #1 (Original Location): The original King Taco, opened in 1974 by Lupe Serrano out of a converted ice cream truck. The beloved LA chain is seeking historic designation for this founding location.

del-rey.Del Rey Square: A modern senior living community that often hosts neighborhood civic meetings and local council events.

downey.10311 Lakewood Boulevard Downey, Califor: The site of the World's Oldest Operating McDonald's, a 1953 walk-up stand that retains its original golden arches and features a museum.
downey.10924 Paramount Blvd: Nordic Fox Coffee Shop - Great Decor.
downey.11111 Brookshire Avenue Downey, CA 90241: Columbia Memorial Space Science Learning Center - columbiaspacescience.org.
downey.13006 Juniper St, Downey, CA 90242: HIDDEN GHOST TOWN DISCOVERED Experience the spectacular remains of the Los Angeles County "Poor Farm" and early days of Rancho Los Amigos Rehabilitation Center. 4.
downey.7900 Imperial Hwy Downey, CA 90242: Crazy Old-School Grocery-  Beach's Market - ' The ultimate space age suburban city.'.
downey.9559 East Imperial Highway Downey, CA 90: Hully Gully - holds 80's Flashbacks Dance Parties.
downey.Close To You Apartments (Carpenter Sibli: Formerly owned by musical siblings Richard and Karen Carpenter (The Carpenters). Named after their breakthrough hit.
downey.Downey Studios (former NASA/Boeing site): Major film and TV production studio complex located on a former NASA/Boeing manufacturing facility.
downey.Exotical Hawaiian Clothing Store: Hawaiian clothing store on Firestone Blvd.
downey.Harvey's Broiler (Bob's Big Boy Broiler): A masterpiece of Googie design and a monument to the golden age of California drive-in culture. Originally Harvey's Broiler, the structure features the dramatic sweeping rooflines, neon-lit canopies, and kinetic energy that defined the "Space Age" commercial architecture of the 1950s and '60s. After a partial demolition threatened to erase it, preservationists fought to save and restore the building, which now operates as Bob's Big Boy Broiler. Its massive neon sign remains a defining landmark on Florence Avenue and a prime example of mid-century roadside advertising as public art.
downey.Johnie's Broiler (Historic Bob's Big Boy: Historic Googie-style drive-in, formerly a Bob's Big Boy; later operating as Johnie's Broiler. A preserved example of mid-century roadside architecture.
downey.McDonalds Downey (Oldest in the World): Oldest surviving McDonalds in the world; features the original single-arch design.
downey.Original Taco Bell Site: Site of the very first original Taco Bell, opened by Glen Bell in 1962.

downtown-la.100 Center St: This federal facility houses the Los Angeles Immigration Court and various regional offices for the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development.
downtown-la.100 W 1st St: A modern 10-story glass and steel landmark serving as the LAPD Headquarters (Police Administration Building) in the Civic Center.
downtown-la.1015 S Main St: A commercial garment building housing showrooms near the famous Santee Alley.
downtown-la.1202 E Olympic Blvd: A massive industrial warehouse in the Produce District utilized for cold storage and logistics.
downtown-la.127 Japanese Village Plaza Mall: A historic open-air shopping and dining hub in Little Tokyo, famous for its traditional architecture and blue-tiled roofs.
downtown-la.1st and Broadway Civic Center Park: Park/Facility in Civic Center / Downtown LA
downtown-la.2nd Street Tunnel: L.A.s most filmed tunnel, lined with glossy white tiles. Featured in Blade Runner, The Terminator, Independence Day, and countless car commercials.
downtown-la.African American Firefighter Museum: Warehouse District. Housed in the historic Fire Station No. 30, it celebrates the history of Black firefighters in Los Angeles.
downtown-la.Ahmanson Theatre & Mark Taper Forum: Part of the Los Angeles Music Center, these 1967 structures are significant examples of New Formalist architecture.
downtown-la.American Apparel Factory: Fashion District. Historically one of the largest garment factories in the world.
downtown-la.American Hotel: Arts District. A landmark for its street murals and its role in the district’s early arts scene.
downtown-la.Am\u00e9rica Tropical: América Tropical is a powerful and historically controversial work by David Alfaro Siqueiros located on the rooftop of the Italian Hall at Olvera Street. The only mural by the famed Mexican muralist currently in its original location in the United States. Features a political critique of American imperialism, leading to its whitewashing shortly after its 1932 completion. Now protected by a specialized shelter and accessible via an interpretive center for public viewing.
downtown-la.Angel's Flight: The world's shortest incorporated railway, Angel's Flight has shuttled passengers between Hill Street and Bunker Hill since its original construction in 1901. Rebuilt in 1996, the funicular is a beloved symbol of Downtown L.A.'s layered history.
downtown-la.Angels Flight Railway (HCM #4): World's shortest railway; connects Hill Street to Bunker Hill.
downtown-la.AON Center: Once the tallest building in the city, this monolith remains a cornerstone of the Financial District.
downtown-la.Arts District: The Arts District Business Improvement District.
downtown-la.Ashanti Fabrics: Fashion District. An importer of African crafts and fabrics, known for its displays of agbadas and kaftans.
downtown-la.B. Black & Sons: Historic fabric store serving tailors and designers.
downtown-la.Bank of America Plaza: A 55-story skyscraper featuring a significant public plaza and gardens.
downtown-la.Bank of Italy / Giannini Building: HCM #454. A 1922 Neoclassical building that served as the regional headquarters for Amadeo Giannini’s bank.
downtown-la.Banks-Huntley Building: HCM #529. A 1930 Art Deco office tower designed by John Parkinson and Donald B. Parkinson.
downtown-la.Barker Brothers Building: A massive 1926 Renaissance Revival building that served as a flagship furniture showroom.
downtown-la.Baroo: 2026 Best Chef: California James Beard Foundation Award Nominee. Chef Kwang Uh's groundbreaking Korean cuisine in the Arts District, noted for its focus on complex fermentations.
downtown-la.Bendix Aviation Building: Fashion District. A 1929 landmark notable for its massive neon sign and aviation-inspired architecture. It remains a dominant feature of the skyline.
downtown-la.Biddy Mason Memorial Park: Park and memorial dedicated to Biddy Mason, a formerly enslaved woman who became a pioneering Los Angeles real estate entrepreneur and philanthropist.
downtown-la.Biltmore Hotel: Opened in 1923, the Millennium Biltmore is one of the most storied hotels in Los Angeles history. Its grand Beaux-Arts interiors have hosted presidential inaugurations, Academy Award ceremonies, and scenes from countless films. The hotel stands as a monument to the golden age of L.A. civic ambition.
downtown-la.Bixel House: A rare surviving Victorian-era residential structure on the western edge of downtown.
downtown-la.Blackstone's Department Store: HCM #765. A 1917 Classical Revival department store designed by John Parkinson with distinctive "shadowbox" display windows.
downtown-la.Bonaventure Hotel: A 1976 Postmodern landmark designed by John Portman, featuring five cylindrical glass towers.
downtown-la.Bradbury Building (HCM #82): An 1893 National Historic Landmark, the Bradbury Building is one of the most celebrated works of commercial architecture in Los Angeles. Its ornate Victorian atrium—featuring glazed tile, iron railings, and open-cage elevators beneath a soaring skylight—has appeared in countless films, including Blade Runner.
downtown-la.Broadway Theater District:
The History: Between 1910 and 1931, this six-block stretch of Broadway was the entertainment capital of the world. It contains the highest concentration of historic movie palaces in the United States.

Key Architectural Features: A designated National Historic District, it features masterpieces like the Eastern Columbia Building, the Los Angeles Theatre, and the United Artists Theatre (now the United Theater on Broadway). The facades are a riot of Beaux-Arts, Art Deco, and Spanish Renaissance styles.
downtown-la.Brock and Company Jewelry Store: A 1922 Renaissance Revival commercial building designed by Dodd & Richards for a premier Los Angeles jeweler.
downtown-la.Bullock's Annex (Gennett Building): Another key auxiliary structure for the Bullock's retail operations, built in the 1920s.
downtown-la.Bullock's Annex (Mackay / Hart Buildings: A series of early 20th-century satellite buildings used for the expansion of the Bullock's department store empire.
downtown-la.Bullock's Annex (Perse Building): Part of the cluster of Hill Street buildings that supported the massive Bullock's department store.
downtown-la.Bunker Hill: The city's corporate and cultural acropolis. Once a neighborhood of Victorian mansions connected by the Angels Flight railway, the hill was leveled and redeveloped in the mid-20th century into a soaring plaza of skyscrapers and world-class arts institutions, including the Walt Disney Concert Hall.
downtown-la.California Club Building: A 1929 Italian Renaissance-style private social club building that has been a bastion of the Los Angeles business and civic establishment for a century. Its architectural elegance exemplifies the ambitions of early 20th-century downtown Los Angeles.
downtown-la.Carver Apartments: Skid Row Area. An award-winning residential building designed by Michael Maltzan for the Skid Row Housing Trust.
downtown-la.Casey\u2019s Irish Pub: Classic underground Irish pub in the heart of Downtown.
downtown-la.Clifton's Republic: A massive downtown venue featuring artificial redwood forests and dioramas; a pioneer of 'fantasy' cafeteria design.
downtown-la.Coca-Cola Building: Warehouse District. A 1939 Streamline Moderne masterpiece designed by Robert V. Derrah to resemble an ocean liner.
downtown-la.Colburn School of Performing Arts: A world-class institute for music and dance education located on Bunker Hill.
downtown-la.Cole\u2019s French Dip (Est. 1908): Located in the historic Pacific Electric Building, Cole's is the other claimant to the French Dip invention and remains one of the oldest public houses in the city.
downtown-la.Continental Building: HCM #153. Completed in 1904, it was the city's first skyscraper and established the 150-foot height limit.
downtown-la.Criminal Courts Building: A 1972 high-rise justice center representing the Corporate International style.
downtown-la.DTLA Alliance: Formerly the Downtown Center BID, which includes Bunker Hill and the Financial District.
downtown-la.East City Hall Controller\u2019s Room: A high-level administrative meeting space located within the iconic Los Angeles City Hall East complex.
downtown-la.Eastern Columbia Building: Often referred to as the 'Jewel of Downtown', this 13-story tower originally served as the headquarters for the Eastern and Columbia Outfitting Companies. Built in 1930 and designed by Claud Beelman in the Art Deco (Zigzag Moderne) style, it is officially Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument #294. The building is world-renowned for its striking, glossy turquoise terracotta facade trimmed with deep blue and gold leaf, capped off by a spectacular four-sided clock tower emblazoned with the word 'EASTERN' in bright neon. In 2006, the building underwent a massive $30 million adaptive reuse project, converting the space into luxury lofts.
downtown-la.Eastern Columbia Building (HCM #224): Historic Core. A 1930 Art Deco masterpiece clad in turquoise terra cotta and gold leaf.
downtown-la.El Rey Hotel: A 1920s hotel that played a significant role in the residential development of the Skid Row district.
downtown-la.Electronics District: This is an informal designation for a cluster of wholesale electronics businesses located within the Los Angeles Downtown Industrial District (LADID) BID. While the BID recognizes the presence of these specialized businesses, the "Electronics District" is not a formal legal entity.
downtown-la.EY Plaza: A skyscraper located directly above the FIGat7th shopping center.
downtown-la.Fashion District / Warehouse District Wa:
The History: The industrial core of Downtown LA.

The Character: This massive 1920s-era brick warehouse is a premier example of the city's early industrial architecture. Today, many of these structures have been converted into high-end photo studios and creative "maker spaces," bridging the gap between LA’s manufacturing past and its creative future.
downtown-la.Fashion District BID: Includes Santee Alley and the Flower District.
downtown-la.Fine Arts Building: HCM #125. A 1926 Romanesque Revival building famous for its lobby with a fountain, murals, and bronze sculptures.
downtown-la.Fire Station No. 23: A historic Los Angeles Fire Department station serving the Skid Row and eastern downtown area, reflecting the civic infrastructure built to serve a rapidly expanding early 20th-century L.A.
downtown-la.Fire Station No. 9: A 1920s Italian Renaissance Revival fire station serving the industrial core.
downtown-la.Firehouse No. 23 (Ghostbusters): The historic, ornate fire station whose interiors were used as the Ghostbusters headquarters (as well as seen in The Mask).
downtown-la.Flower District: Specialized District. Featuring the largest flower market in the United States.
downtown-la.Foreman and Clark Building: HCM #913. A 1929 Gothic Revival/Art Deco skyscraper designed by Curlett & Beelman.
downtown-la.Freehand L.A. (Commercial Exchange): Hotel housed in the historic Commercial Exchange Building.
downtown-la.Furniture & Decorative Arts District: Often referred to as the "Furniture District," this area typically falls within the Fashion District BID or LADID. It is a subset of the broader wholesale corridor rather than a standalone BID.
downtown-la.Gas Co Tower: An award-winning office tower recognized for its blue-tinted glass and stepped crown.
downtown-la.Grand Central Market: An iconic 30,000-square-foot food hall operating continuously since 1917.
downtown-la.Hamburger's Department Store: Later known as the May Company, this 1908 Beaux-Arts building was once the largest department store in the West.
downtown-la.Harbor Freeway Overture: Harbor Freeway Overture is a hyper-realistic mural by Kent Twitchell that overlooks the 110 Freeway. Features oversized portraits of twelve musicians from the Los Angeles Chamber Orchestra. Designed specifically to be "read" by motorists traveling at freeway speeds. A hallmark of Twitchell's style, which elevates everyday or local figures to monumental status.
downtown-la.Hauser & Wirth (Globe Mills): International contemporary art gallery housed in the restored Globe Mills complex.
downtown-la.Hippie Kitchen: Skid Row Area. A long-standing Catholic Worker soup kitchen serving the local community.
downtown-la.Historic Core BID: The Historic Core Business Improvement District.
downtown-la.Hotel Cecil: A 1924 hotel that remains a significant example of large-scale commercial hotel development in early DTLA.
downtown-la.Industrial District BID (LADID): The Los Angeles Downtown Industrial District Business Improvement District.
downtown-la.Inner City Arts: Skid Row Area. A campus providing arts education to underserved youth.
downtown-la.Japanese Village Plaza: Little Tokyo. A central shopping and dining hub.
downtown-la.John Ferraro Building (DWP): HCM #1022. A 1965 Mid-Century Modern masterpiece surrounded by a reflecting pool.
downtown-la.Kato: 2025 Best Chef: California James Beard Foundation Award Winner. Chef Jon Yao's celebrated Taiwanese-inspired tasting menu located in the ROW DTLA complex.
downtown-la.Kenneth Hahn Hall of Administration: A 1960 New Formalist building that serves as the seat of County government.
downtown-la.L.A. County Law Library: Major public law library, housed in a prominent Mid-Century Modern building.
downtown-la.La Iglesia de Nuestra Se\u00f1ora Reina: El Pueblo. The oldest church in the city, located in the historic El Pueblo district.
downtown-la.LAPD Police Administration Building: Civic Center. A modern civic landmark.
downtown-la.Loew's State Theatre: A 1921 theater and office building that was once the largest brick-clad structure in Los Angeles.
downtown-la.Los Angeles Athletic Club: Founded in 1880, the Los Angeles Athletic Club is one of the city's oldest private clubs. Its 1912 building is a distinguished example of Beaux-Arts architecture in the downtown core, having trained Olympic athletes and hosted civic leaders for over a century.
downtown-la.Los Angeles Central Library: Designed by Bertram Goodhue and completed in 1926, the Central Library is one of the great civic monuments of Los Angeles. Its distinctive Egyptian-Revival tower and Goodhue's allegorical murals represent the city's aspirations during its first great boom. Expanded by Hardy Holzman Pfeiffer in 1993 after a devastating 1986 arson.
downtown-la.Los Angeles Central Library (HCM #46): Egyptian and Mediterranean influences; features the Tom Bradley Wing.
downtown-la.Los Angeles City Hall: HCM #150. A 1928 Art Deco/Beaux-Arts landmark and the iconic symbol of the city's government.
downtown-la.Los Angeles City Hall (HCM #150): Iconic Art Deco tower; tallest building in LA until 1964.
downtown-la.Los Angeles County Hall of Records: Designed by Richard Neutra and Robert Alexander (1962), a masterpiece of Modernism.
downtown-la.Los Angeles Hall of Records: Civic Center. Known for its unique architecture and the underground tunnel connecting it to the Hall of Administration.
downtown-la.Los Angeles Stock Exchange: HCM #239. A 1931 Art Deco masterpiece by Samuel Lunden featuring elaborate bronze doors and granite carvings.
downtown-la.Los Angeles Stock Exchange (Former): An earlier 1920s location of the exchange before the 1931 monument was completed.
downtown-la.Los Angeles Theatre: HCM #225. A 1931 French Baroque movie palace designed by S. Charles Lee; widely considered one of the most opulent theaters in the world.
downtown-la.Los Angeles Theatre (HCM #225):
Architect: S. Charles Lee

Architectural Style: French Baroque

The History: Built in just six months to be ready for the premiere of Charlie Chaplin’s City Lights, this was the most expensive and opulent theater built on Broadway. Chaplin himself reportedly helped fund the completion when the developer ran out of money.

Key Architectural Features: The lobby was modeled after the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles. It features a massive crystal chandelier, a grand fountain, and a sprawling basement lounge with a glass-walled ballroom.
downtown-la.Maple Center: Maple Center, a two-story wholesale shopping hub located in the heart of the Fashion District.
downtown-la.Mayan Theater: HCM #460. Designed by Morgan, Walls & Clements in 1927, this is a premier example of Mayan Revival architecture featuring intricate pre-Columbian motifs.
downtown-la.Mega Toys: Arts District / Toy District. Founded by Charlie Woo, an immigrant from Hong Kong, this business is credited with the development of the surrounding Toy Town.
downtown-la.Metro Central Maintenance Facility: Located just down the street from the Twin Towers, this massive, utilitarian complex serves as the absolute nerve center for the Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority's (Metro) bus fleet. While tourists rarely venture here, this facility is vital to the daily functioning of the city. It is the primary heavy-maintenance hub where Metro's massive fleet of buses are repaired, painted, and overhauled to keep the sprawling transit network moving.
downtown-la.MOCA (Museum of Contemporary Art): The premier museum in Los Angeles dedicated solely to contemporary art.
downtown-la.Modernica Warehouse: Arts District. A sprawling facility for the well-known furniture designer.
downtown-la.New Girl Loft (The Binford Building): The exterior building used as the hipster Arts District loft for Jess and her roommates in the sitcom New Girl.
downtown-la.Nishi Hongwanji Buddhist Temple: Little Tokyo. A historic religious landmark.
downtown-la.NoMad Los Angeles / Hotel Per La: Luxury hotel located in the historic 1920s Giannini Place building.
downtown-la.Olympic Auditorium: A 1925 grand arena that served as a major venue for boxing, wrestling, and the 1932 Olympic Games.
downtown-la.One Bunker Hill (S. California Edison): HCM #347. A 1931 Art Deco building known for its spectacular "power-themed" murals and lobby.
downtown-la.One Wilshire: A 1966 International Style tower that has become one of the most important telecommunications hubs in the world.
downtown-la.Original Pantry Caf\u00e9: HCM #1017. A 1924 restaurant building that is a cultural landmark for its continuous 24/7 operation since opening.
downtown-la.Orpheum Theatre:
Architect: G. Albert Lansburgh

Architectural Style: Beaux-Arts

The History: The fourth and final home for the Orpheum vaudeville circuit in LA, this theater has hosted everyone from Judy Garland and Jack Benny to Aretha Franklin and Little Richard. It is widely considered the most beautifully restored theater on Broadway.

Key Architectural Features: The French Renaissance-inspired interior features a mighty Wurlitzer pipe organ (one of only three remaining on the West Coast) and a grand lobby with marble floors and gold-leafed ceilings.
downtown-la.Ozawa Boarding House: Historic boarding house in Little Tokyo.
downtown-la.Palace Theater: HCM #348. Completed in 1911, it is the oldest surviving Orpheum vaudeville house in the United States.
downtown-la.Palace Theatre:
Architect: G. Albert Lansburgh

Architectural Style: French Renaissance

The History: Originally the Orpheum (the predecessor to the 1926 Orpheum down the street), this is the oldest remaining Orpheum vaudeville theater in the United States.

Key Architectural Features: The facade is notable for its multicolored terracotta and four figures representing the muses of Vaudeville. The interior is unique for its "steep" balcony design, which allowed every seat to have a clear view of the stage performers.
downtown-la.Palm Court (Alexandria Hotel): The ornate lobby and Palm Court of the 1906 Alexandria Hotel was once the social center of Los Angeles, where film stars, oil barons, and presidents gathered. A critical piece of the Spring Street financial district, now recognized as the 'Wall Street of the West.'
downtown-la.Paul\u2019s Kitchen: Long-running historic Chinese restaurant in the City Market area.
downtown-la.Philharmonic Auditorium (Site of): Former site of the Clune's Auditorium (later Philharmonic Auditorium), a major cultural venue that hosted the Los Angeles Philharmonic and many landmark events from 1906 until its demolition in 1985.
downtown-la.Philippe The Original: Established in 1908, this is one of the oldest restaurants in Los Angeles and the self-proclaimed inventor of the French Dip sandwich. It is a cornerstone of the historic core near Union Station and a must-visit for any L.A. historian.
downtown-la.Pi\u00f1ata District: This is a culturally significant hub along East Olympic Boulevard near South Central Avenue. It is a recognized community name but functions as an informal enclave within the LADID or Fashion District boundaries rather than an official administrative sub-area.
downtown-la.Produce District: This is a recognized functional hub hosted within the LADID BID, which encompasses the Los Angeles Wholesale Produce Market. Historically, the Downtown Los Angeles Neighborhood Council (DLANC) has referred to this area as the Produce District in its planning documents.
downtown-la.Regent Theatre: Historic theater on Main St, operating as a live music and performance venue.
downtown-la.Roxie Theater: Built in 1931, this was the last of the major theaters built on the Broadway corridor, notable for its Art Deco style.
downtown-la.Saint Vibiana's Cathedral: Built in 1876, Saint Vibiana's was the first Cathedral of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Los Angeles. Damaged in the 1994 Northridge Earthquake, it was decommissioned and controversially saved from demolition. Now restored as Vibiana, an event and arts venue while preserving its remarkable Baroque facade.
downtown-la.Saint Vincent Court: Hidden in a Downtown LA alleyway, this is a bizarre and beautiful slice of old Europe featuring cobbled pavement, ornate streetlamps, and faux-European storefronts perfect for a quiet coffee break.
downtown-la.Santee Alley: Fashion District. A high-density retail corridor known for its flea-market atmosphere.
downtown-la.Scrap City / Scrap District: Specialized District. Industrial area characterized by recycling yards and industrial detritus.
downtown-la.Seafood District: Similar to the Produce District, this is a specialized hub of seafood wholesalers located within the boundaries of the LADID BID.
downtown-la.Senor Fish: Little Tokyo. A popular local eatery in the heart of the district.
downtown-la.Skid Row (Central City East):
The History: Historically known as Central City East, the term "Skid Row" dates back to the late 19th century when the area served as the end of the line for the railroad, attracting transient workers and low-cost hotels.

The Context: Today, it is a complex 50-block area that contains one of the largest stable populations of homeless individuals in the United States. It is a site of intense social struggle, but also a hub for vital non-profits like the Midnight Mission and the Union Rescue Mission.
downtown-la.South Park BID: The South Park Business Improvement District.
downtown-la.State Theatre (Loew's State):
Architect: Weeks & Day

Architectural Style: Spanish Renaissance

The History: Located at the busy corner of 7th and Broadway, this was once the West Coast flagship for the Loew's theater circuit. It functioned as both a vaudeville and movie house.

Key Architectural Features: The theater is part of a larger 12-story office building. The auditorium features intricate "plateresque" Spanish detailing and was famously built with a tunnel connecting it to the nearby athletic club for performers.
downtown-la.Sun Realty Building: HCM #389. A 1931 Art Deco tower noted for its distinctive turquoise terra-cotta cladding.
downtown-la.The Abbey (San Pedro St): Skid Row Area. A historic mission-style structure in the area.
downtown-la.The Biltmore Hotel (HCM #60): Beaux-Arts style; historic site of the founding of the Academy Awards.
downtown-la.The Bloc: A transformed open-air destination featuring retail, the Sheraton Grand, and office space.
downtown-la.The Broad: A contemporary art museum known for its unique honeycomb "veil" exterior.
downtown-la.The CalEdison: A historic Art Deco building formerly known as the One Bunker Hill building.
downtown-la.The Castle (Site of): Former site of one of Bunker Hill's most photographed grand Victorian mansions. Like its neighbors, The Castle fell to the redevelopment bulldozers of the 1960s, erasing an entire chapter of fin-de-siècle Los Angeles residential life.
downtown-la.The Cecil Hotel (Stay on Main): A focal point for 'dark tourism' in Downtown LA, this hotel has a grueling history as the home of serial killers Richard Ramirez (The Night Stalker) and Jack Unterweger. It gained worldwide notoriety for the 2013 Elisa Lam mystery. Its looming neon sign remains a dark landmark on the edge of Skid Row.
downtown-la.The Doughboy: A WWI memorial statue located in Pershing Square, dedicated in 1924.
downtown-la.The Factory Place Arts Complex: The Factory Place Arts Complex, a historic warehouse converted into a creative live-work community in the heart of the Arts District.
downtown-la.The Family of Man: A 1960s public sculpture installation located at City Hall East.
downtown-la.The Maple Business Center: The Maple Business Center, a major high-rise hub for the city’s apparel industry.
downtown-la.The Pope of Broadway: The Pope of Broadway is a 70-foot-tall mural by Eloy Torrez that serves as a landmark for the Historic Core’s theatrical heritage. Depicts actor Anthony Quinn in a celebratory dance, echoing his role in Zorba the Greek. Located on the side of the Victor Clothing Building, visible from blocks away. Underwent a major restoration in 2017 to bring back its original vibrant colors and detail.
downtown-la.The Proper Hotel: The Proper Hotel, featuring interiors by Kelly Wearstler within a restored 1920s Renaissance Revival building.
downtown-la.The Salt Box (Site of): Former site of one of Bunker Hill's most iconic Victorian-era houses, nicknamed for its distinctive shape. The structure was demolished during the Bunker Hill urban renewal project of the 1960s, which reshaped the entire neighborhood.
downtown-la.The Smell: Historic Core. A long-running, non-profit, all-ages volunteer-run punk and experimental music venue.
downtown-la.The South Park Center: The South Park Center, a 32-story modernist office tower designed by architect William Pereira.
downtown-la.The Trust Building: Historic Art Deco office building and architectural landmark.
downtown-la.Title Insurance Building: HCM #772. A 1928 Zigzag Moderne building designed by Parkinson & Parkinson, featuring a spectacular lobby.
downtown-la.Tower Theatre:
Architect: S. Charles Lee

Architectural Style: Spanish Renaissance / Neo-Moorish

The History: This was the first theater in Los Angeles wired for "talking pictures" (sound). After decades of neglect, it underwent a high-profile restoration in 2021.

Modern Era: The building now houses a flagship Apple Store. The restoration preserved the grand staircase, the stained glass window featuring a film strip, and the ornate plasterwork, making it a premier example of commercial adaptive reuse.
downtown-la.Track 16 Gallery: A contemporary art gallery in the Fashion District.
downtown-la.Twin Towers Correctional Facility: Authorized and constructed after the 1994 Northridge earthquake heavily damaged the historic Hall of Justice jail, this massive 1997 Panoptic Podular Design complex is operated by the LA County Sheriff's Department. Spanning 10 acres, the facility holds a heavy, dual-purpose distinction: it is simultaneously known as the world's largest jail facility and the world's largest mental health facility, processing hundreds of detainees every single day.
downtown-la.Two California Plaza (2Cal): The taller sister tower to 1Cal, serving as a pillar of the DTLA skyline.
downtown-la.U.S. Bank Tower: A dominant feature of the skyline, originally known as the Library Tower.
downtown-la.Union Station: The main railway station in Los Angeles, known as the Last of the Great Railway Stations, combining Art Deco, Mission Revival, and Streamline Moderne styles.
downtown-la.United Artists Theater Building: HCM #435. A 1927 Spanish Gothic theater and office tower built for the film studio founded by Mary Pickford, Douglas Fairbanks, and Charlie Chaplin.
downtown-la.United Artists Theatre: Historic Core. Now the Los Angeles University Cathedral, this Spanish Gothic building is iconic for its "Jesus Saves" neon signs.
downtown-la.Walt Disney Concert Hall: A Frank Gehry-designed architectural landmark and home to the LA Philharmonic.
downtown-la.Warner Music Group: Warner Music Group offices in the transformed Ford Factory building.
downtown-la.Washington Boulevard Corridor: This refers to the southern boundary of the Fashion District BID. While it is a specific zone for city-wide projects and BID services, it is an administrative boundary line rather than a named sub-district.
downtown-la.Wayne's World Club Location: Arts District. A warehouse space used as a filming location for the club scenes in the film Wayne's World.
downtown-la.Wells Fargo Center: A landmark twin-tower office complex located at the peak of Bunker Hill.
downtown-la.Wilshire Grand Center: The tallest building in the Western United States, topped by a sail-shaped spire.
downtown-la.Wolfer Printing Co. Building: Skid Row Area. A 1928 structure designed by Edward C. and Ellis W. Taylor.
downtown-la.Zenshuji Soto Mission: Little Tokyo. The first Soto Zen temple founded in North America.

eagle-rock.Azteca Tire & Auto Repair: Local auto repair shop on Eagle Rock Blvd.
eagle-rock.Bon Temps Cottages (True Blood filming l: Residential cottages featured as the exterior of Merlotte's Bar in the television show True Blood.
eagle-rock.Brad Spence Studio: Artist studio on Eagle Vista Drive.
eagle-rock.Casa Lu-An Motel: Classic mid-century motel on Colorado Blvd.
eagle-rock.Cindy's Coffee Shop: Local coffee shop and neighborhood gathering spot.
eagle-rock.Conex Development Co (Giant Chess Piece): Commercial office featuring a giant chess piece sculpture.
eagle-rock.Eagle Rock (The Landmark): The Eagle Rock formation is a natural volcanic rock outcropping that gives the neighborhood its name. The shadow of a giant eagle is cast on its face at certain times of day, a phenomenon observed by indigenous peoples for millennia before the arrival of European settlers.
eagle-rock.Eagle Rock City Hall: The former municipal center of the independent city of Eagle Rock before it was annexed by Los Angeles in 1923. The building now serves civic functions and stands as a reminder of Eagle Rock's brief history as an autonomous city.
eagle-rock.Eagle Rock Mall (Fil-Mart & Goldilocks): Strip mall featuring Fil-Mart and Goldilocks Bake Shop, a hub of the Filipino community.
eagle-rock.Eagle Rock Yacht Club: Local watering hole humorously named the Eagle Rock Yacht Club — despite being landlocked.
eagle-rock.Edwards & Wildey Building: Historic commercial building on Colorado Blvd.
eagle-rock.El Bioston / Sight Waves Autosound: Local commercial address on Colorado Blvd.
eagle-rock.Ernie Jr.'s (Mexican Restaurant): Mexican restaurant with Henry's Tacos-style font signage.
eagle-rock.Excessive Satellite Dishes Residence: Residential property noted for an unusually excessive number of satellite dishes.
eagle-rock.Garden of Believing: Unique community garden and public art installation on Colorado Blvd.
eagle-rock.Hal's Eagle Rock Pet Shop: Long-standing local pet shop on Eagle Rock Blvd.
eagle-rock.Hanson Puthuff House: One of the oldest homes in the Eagle Rock area.
eagle-rock.Hubert Barber Shop: Local barbershop on Eagle Rock Blvd.
eagle-rock.In The Red Records - Larry Hardy Home: Home of In The Red Records owner Larry Hardy, an influential independent punk and garage rock label.
eagle-rock.Jay Duplass Residence: Residence of writer, director, and actor Jay Duplass.
eagle-rock.Laser Coin Car Wash: Local car wash on Colorado Blvd.
eagle-rock.Leaving Records: Independent record label and music shop on El Paso Dr.
eagle-rock.Life In A Tipi Residence: Residential curiosity — a property where the occupants notably lived in a tipi.

eagle-rock.Not Not Fun Records: Influential indie/experimental music label on Loleta Ave.
eagle-rock.Old Googie Sign at Nese Realty: Surviving Googie-era sign at a local realty office on Eagle Rock Blvd.
eagle-rock.Pat & Lorraine's Coffee Shop: Classic Eagle Rock coffee shop; a neighborhood institution.
eagle-rock.Pete's Blue Chip Burgers: Classic burger joint on Colorado Blvd.
eagle-rock.Philippine Village Center: Filipino community commercial center on Eagle Rock Blvd.
eagle-rock.Queen's Arms: Local bar and neighborhood gathering spot on York Blvd.
eagle-rock.Rambo's Taco Truck: Popular local taco truck on Eagle Rock Blvd.
eagle-rock.Renaissance Arts Academy (Pillers Buildi: Arts academy housed in the historic Pillers building on Argus Dr.
eagle-rock.Rock Row Green Condominiums: Modern eco-conscious condominiums on Yosemite Drive.
eagle-rock.Siglio Press: Publisher at the intersection of art and literature, based in Eagle Rock.
eagle-rock.Stained Glass Supplies: Stained glass supply shop that has been in business for decades.
eagle-rock.The Bucket Burger Stand (est. 1935): Historic 'rickety old shack' burger stand in continuous operation since 1935.
eagle-rock.The Coffee Table Bistro: Beloved neighborhood bistro and coffee spot on Colorado Blvd.
eagle-rock.The Eagle Rock: A massive natural rock formation and sacred site with a distinctive silhouette of an eagle in flight, visible from the 134 freeway.
eagle-rock.The Farley Building (Mike Kelley's Studi: Site of artist Mike Kelley's studio, a significant location in the LA art world.
eagle-rock.The Oinkster: Popular local burger and pastrami restaurant on Colorado Blvd.
eagle-rock.Tritch Hardware: Long-standing local hardware store on Colorado Blvd.
eagle-rock.Unique Architecture Residence (Norwalk A: Notable residential building with unusual and distinctive architecture.
eagle-rock.Video Drive-In (VHS Club): Club dedicated to screening 'insane' and obscure VHS films.
eagle-rock.Welcome Inn (Art Festival Site): Motel known as a regular site for community art festivals.
eagle-rock.Women's Twentieth Century Club: Historic women's club and social institution on Hermosa Ave.
eagle-rock.Yosemite Studios (former location): Former location of Yosemite Studios, used by artists including Souther Salazar.

east-covina.Jack Horner's House (Boogie Nights): Tucked away in the South Hills area of Covina, this property served as the luxurious home and primary party hub for adult film director Jack Horner in Paul Thomas Anderson's Boogie Nights (1997). The property is mostly hidden from the street today.

east-hollywood.1319 N La Brea Ave: A Hollywood commercial site that formerly served as the green room for Jimmy Kimmel Live!.
east-hollywood.Charles Bukowski's Home: The former residence of the prolific L.A. poet and novelist.
east-hollywood.El Potrillo (Horse on the Roof): Mexican restaurant on N Virgil Ave featuring a distinctive horse sculpture on the roof.
east-hollywood.Hollywood High School: Hollywood High School, a historic educational landmark known for its famous alumni and massive campus murals.
east-hollywood.Iglesia Manantial Redencion: A community landmark with distinctive architecture.
east-hollywood.J.M. Close Egyptian Revival Apartments (: A series of 1920s Egyptian-inspired buildings, including the 1926 Ahmed Apartments and the 1925 Karnak Apartments.
east-hollywood.Kuya Lord: 2024 Best Chef: California James Beard Foundation Award Winner. Chef Lord Maynard Llera's masterful regional Filipino cuisine, famous for its slow-roasted Lucenachon.
east-hollywood.LA Fresh Poultry: Local fresh poultry market on N Virgil Ave.
east-hollywood.La Luz De Jesus Gallery: A pioneering gallery of lowbrow, pop surrealism, and underground art.
east-hollywood.Laundryland (Fight Club Site): A Churrigueresque-style laundromat used as a filming location for Fight Club.
east-hollywood.Lemon Grove Recreation Center: A neighborhood park hub providing essential open space and athletics for the East Hollywood/Melrose Hill area.
east-hollywood.Sahag's Basturma Sandwich Shop: An unassuming Armenian institution specializing in basturma (cured beef), owned by Harry Tashyan.
east-hollywood.Sapp Coffee Shop: A legendary destination in Thai Town, frequently cited for its authentic boat noodles.
east-hollywood.Smog Cutter: Beloved dive bar on N Virgil Ave; featured in Elliott Smiths music video for Miss Misery.
east-hollywood.Trianon Apartments: Built in 1928 by Douglas Fairbanks Jr. and Mary Pickford.
east-hollywood.Virgil Village Residential (137 N Virgil: Residential address in the Virgil Village area of East Hollywood.
east-hollywood.Wat Mai Thai Town Temple: A central spiritual hub for the Thai community in East Hollywood.

east-hollywood---hel-mel.East Hollywood - Hel-Mel:
The Character: This vibrant micro-neighborhood, centered at the intersection of Heliotrope Drive and Melrose Avenue, is one of East Hollywood’s most creative pockets. It is defined by its bike-friendly culture, independent spirit, and colorful storefronts.

Key Features: Long known as a hub for cyclists (anchored by the famous Orange 20 Bikes), the area features an array of artisanal coffee shops, vintage clothing boutiques, and independent eateries. It represents a more grassroots, community-oriented version of the "cool LA" aesthetic found in neighboring Silver Lake or Los Feliz.

east-los-angeles.\"Thriller\" Dance Routine Site: The industrial backlot of the Eastside provided the perfect gritty atmosphere for the iconic dance sequence in Michael Jackson’s Thriller video.
east-los-angeles.Belvedere Gardens:
The History: Developed in the early 1920s, Belvedere Gardens was originally marketed as an affordable suburban tract for working-class families. It predates the incorporation of much of the surrounding area and holds a vital place in the cultural history of the Mexican-American community in Los Angeles.

Neighborhood Character: Today, it is part of the heart of East LA. The area is defined by its dense residential streets, historic single-family bungalows, and its proximity to the historic Belvedere Park, which remains a primary gathering space for community events and regional sports.
east-los-angeles.Belvedere Park (Belvedere Gardens): Anchoring the historic Belvedere Gardens tract, this park is a massive recreational oasis featuring a lake and a large outdoor amphitheater. It blends lush green space with the storied history of one of the county’s earliest residential developments, serving as a primary gathering spot for the residents of the unincorporated Eastside.
east-los-angeles.Brown Beret Mural: A vital piece of political history, documenting the legacy of the Brown Berets and the Chicano Moratorium. It serves as a visual reminder of the 1968 "Blowouts" or "Walkouts" where students fought for educational equity.
east-los-angeles.City Terrace Park: Perched atop the neighborhood's characteristic steep hills, this park is the literal and figurative heart of City Terrace, offering sweeping, unobstructed views of the Downtown Los Angeles skyline. It is a vibrant cultural stage famous for its community murals and terraced landscapes that echo the dramatic, winding topography of the surrounding "East Los" hills.
east-los-angeles.Disappearing Virgin Mary Mural: A poignant example of the religious vernacular in the Eastside. The imagery of the Virgin Mary is ubiquitous here, often appearing at the intersection of faith and street art.
east-los-angeles.East Los Angeles Rising Youth Club: A community youth club providing crucial foundational support.
east-los-angeles.El Aguila Bakery: Historic local bakery serving traditional Mexican pan dulce and pastries.
east-los-angeles.El Barrio Free Clinic: Historic site of the El Barrio Free Clinic on Whittier Blvd.
east-los-angeles.El Gallo Bakery: Founded in 1949 by Magdalena Martinez-Huerta and her brother Lauro, this bakery is a Belvedere Gardens landmark. It is renowned for its authentic pan dulce, particularly its "razor-sharp" conchas, empanadas, and cuernitos de mantequilla. The interior is a design time capsule, featuring the original wooden display cases from the 1940s. The "Calendar" Legacy: A specific point of pride for the bakery is its annual calendar. For decades, El Gallo has distributed calendars featuring classic Mexican artwork (often Aztec or religious imagery) that have become staple decorations in kitchens across East Los Angeles, serving as a year-long reminder of the bakery's role as a cultural repository.
east-los-angeles.El Mercadito: Built in 1968, this massive, tri-level indoor marketplace on the East LA / Boyle Heights border was directly modeled after the traditional bustling mercados of Mexico. It was designed to be a comprehensive hub for the community to shop, eat, and gather, complete with roaming mariachi bands and a highly festive atmosphere. Rather than a single restaurant, this is a sprawling culinary destination featuring dozens of independent food stalls, bakeries, and vendors—the perfect place to explore an enormous variety of authentic regional Mexican fare under one roof.
east-los-angeles.El Pedorrero Muffler Shop: Described locally as the "Circus of Auto Repair Shops," it represents the vibrant, high-energy commercial aesthetic of the Eastside, where business facades are often as loud and colorful as a carnival.
east-los-angeles.El Pino (The Pine Tree): A massive Bunya pine made famous by the film 'Blood In, Blood Out'; a living landmark of Eastside identity.
east-los-angeles.El Pino (The Pine): (At southeastern corner of Folsom St & N. Indiana St). A massive Bunya pine tree that has achieved legendary status due to its role in the film Blood In, Blood Out. It serves as a navigational North Star for the neighborhood and a symbol of rootedness.
east-los-angeles.El Tepeyac Cafe (Manuel\u2019s Special): Perhaps the most famous restaurant in the Eastside, this family-run landmark (est. 1955) is the birthplace of the massive "Manuel’s Special" burrito. It is a site of culinary pilgrimage, known for its bustling, old-school atmosphere and a menu that has satisfied generations of locals and visitors with its rich, slow-simmered chile verde.
east-los-angeles.Eugene A. Obregon Park: Commemorative park named for Eugene A. Obregon, a Mexican American Marine who posthumously received the Medal of Honor for his actions in the Korean War.
east-los-angeles.Garfield High School: Historic high school, famously known as the site of the 1968 East L.A. Walkouts and Jaime Escalante’s AP Calculus program (Stand and Deliver).
east-los-angeles.Golden Gate Theatre: A 1927 Spanish Churrigueresque masterpiece. It is one of the most stunning buildings in the city, though it now serves as a retail space (CVS), preserving the shell of its former glory.
east-los-angeles.Jim's Burgers: Representing the iconic "Greek-owned burger stand" culture of Southern California, Jim’s is an essential part of the Eastside's car-culture history. Known for its massive portions, breakfast burritos, and charbroiled burgers, it is a quintessential 24-hour community anchor.
east-los-angeles.King Taco (The Original Location): While now a massive chain, King Taco was born in East LA in 1974 when founder Raul Martínez converted an ice cream truck into a mobile taco stand. The East 3rd Street location is a cultural monument to the modern LA taco scene, credited with helping to popularize the "street taco" format that defines the city today.
east-los-angeles.La Azteca Tortilleria: A beloved, old-school Eastside institution that has been in operation for over 65 years. Before the awards and massive lines, it started strictly as a neighborhood tortilleria (tortilla bakery). Today, it is revered by food critics and locals alike as serving some of the absolute best burritos in the country. Their secret weapon is their handmade flour tortillas—blistered, soft, and impossibly light. Their undisputed masterpiece is the Chile Relleno Burrito, featuring a freshly battered and fried cheese-stuffed poblano pepper wrapped directly into the tortilla with refried beans and pico de gallo.
east-los-angeles.Latin Playboy Nightclub (Former Site): Site of the former Latin Playboy Nightclub, a legendary East LA music and entertainment venue.
east-los-angeles.Los 5 Puntos: Established in 1967, this bustling corner market and deli (named for the five-point intersection it sits on) has been a vital culinary anchor for the community for decades. It feels less like a traditional restaurant and more like a high-energy, authentic carnicería. Meat is the absolute specialty here, fiercely renowned for their carnitas (braised pork), which are slow-cooked in massive copper cazos right behind the glass counter and served on thick, fresh handmade corn tortillas perfectly griddled to order.
east-los-angeles.Los Cinco Puntos: Located at the historic "five points" intersection, this deli and meat market is a sensory masterpiece. It is legendary for its hand-pressed, thick corn tortillas made right before your eyes and its carnitas, which many consider the gold standard of the city. It remains a foundational community hub where the line often stretches out the door for Sunday morning menudo.
east-los-angeles.Lupe\u2019s Taco Stand (Lupe\u2019s Burr: A cornerstone of "East Los" culture since it opened in 1972, Lupe’s is a quintessential roadside walk-up stand that captures the timeless spirit of the Eastside. It is most famous for its "OG" bean and cheese burritos—simple, molten masterpieces of creamy beans and sharp cheddar wrapped in a perfectly toasted flour tortilla. With its classic red-and-white signage and permanent lines of loyal locals, the stand serves as a multi-generational gathering spot where the recipes have remained unchanged for over half a century.
east-los-angeles.Maravilla Handball Court: Built in 1928, this is the oldest handball court in East Los Angeles. It’s more than a sports site; it’s a community anchor that has survived for nearly a century, representing the grit and longevity of the Maravilla neighborhood.
east-los-angeles.Maravilla Handball Court (El Centro Comm: A true architectural "marvel," this historic brick handball court was built in 1928 and stands as one of the oldest and most significant athletic landmarks in the region. It is a living monument to the community’s spirit, serving for nearly a century as a sacred gathering ground where generations of Eastside residents have perfected the fast-paced game of handball.
east-los-angeles.Self-Help Graphics (Former Site): For decades, this was the epicenter of Chicano printmaking and art. Though it has moved, this location is hallowed ground for the Eastside’s creative output, having fostered legends like David Botello and the East Los Streetscrapers.
east-los-angeles.Tacos Baja: A Whittier Boulevard staple that brought the authentic flavors of Ensenada to the Eastside. Famous for its crispy fish and shrimp tacos topped with a signature cream sauce, it stands as a testament to the diverse regional Mexican influences that have taken root in the neighborhood over the decades.
east-los-angeles.Tamales Liliana's: What began as a humble street-corner operation in the late 20th century by Liliana and Juan Manuel Santo has evolved into a "Tamale Empire." The restaurant is a cornerstone of the Eastside's Christmas traditions, famously churning out up to 18,000 tamales a day during the holiday peak. The "American Dream" Story: Beyond their signature red and green pork tamales, the site is a celebrated example of immigrant resilience. The restaurant has expanded over the decades to offer a full menu of "homemade-style" Mexican comfort food, including pozole and menudo, and now anchors a small family-run block that includes Lily's Cafe next door.
east-los-angeles.Tamales Liliana\u2019s: A mandatory stop during the holidays, Liliana’s is synonymous with the tradition of the tamale in East Los Angeles. Whether you are ordering a single pork tamale or a dozen for a family gathering, it represents the soulful, domestic heart of the community's culinary heritage.
east-los-angeles.The Boogie Den: A massive wholesale vintage clothing warehouse that anchors the industrial sector, often frequented by stylists and designers looking for the "Eastside aesthetic."
east-los-angeles.The Boulevard Theatre: A former theater and home to "Huggy Boy" remnants, referencing the legendary DJ who was the voice of the Eastside’s lowrider and oldies culture.
east-los-angeles.The Maravilla Housing Project: Originally established as a residential anchor for the local workforce, this sprawling complex is an essential piece of the social history of East Los Angeles. It represents the mid-century evolution of the neighborhood, marking the transition from early settlements into the foundational, multi-generational community that defines the Maravilla area today.
east-los-angeles.The Tamale (Programmatic Architecture): A rare and glorious example of programmatic architecture. Much like the Tail O' the Pup, this building is shaped like a giant tamale. It is a survivor of the 1920s roadside kitsch era, designed to catch the eye of early motorists on Whittier Blvd.
east-los-angeles.Tony\u2019s Transmissions (Muffler Man): This site features another Muffler Man—a fiberglass giant that serves as a sentinel of the machine-age commercial corridors.
east-los-angeles.United Oil (Googie Elements): Reflects the mid-century 'windshield viewing' design aesthetic found along the Eastside's major thoroughfares.

echo-park.1421 Baxter Street Los Angeles, CA 90026: This residence sits on one of the steepest streets in the world, known for its 32% grade.
echo-park.1430 Cerro Gordo Street, Los Angeles, CA: A hillside residence in Echo Park with a layout optimized for the neighborhood's steep terrain.
echo-park.1433 Avon Park Terrace: A rare surviving cluster of bungalow court living. These represent the "density" of early Elysian Heights, providing affordable, high-quality housing for the artists and writers who flooded the area in the 1920s.
echo-park.1456 Cerro Gordo St: A scenic residential property located on the historic ridges of the northern Echo Park hills.
echo-park.1471 Echo Park Ave., Los Angeles, CA 900: The Echo Park Time Travel Mart, a quirky storefront supporting the 826LA youth writing program.
echo-park.1525 Duane St: A residential property located in the heart of the Echo Park hillside community.
echo-park.1528 Echo Park Ave: A popular commercial address in Echo Park housing local cafes and retail.
echo-park.Allesandro Elementary School: An Echo Park area schoolhouse known for its active community participation and picturesque location near the Silver Lake border.
echo-park.Angelino Heights HPOZ:
Architectural Style: Victorian (Queen Anne and Eastlake)

The History: Angelino Heights was the city’s first designated Historic Preservation Overlay Zone (HPOZ). It was the first true "suburb" of Los Angeles, built on a hill to provide residents with a respite from the dust and noise of the burgeoning downtown.

Key Features: It contains the highest concentration of Victorian-era homes in the city, primarily centered on the world-famous Carroll Avenue. This neighborhood is a living museum of 19th-century architecture and has served as the backdrop for countless films and music videos (including Michael Jackson’s Thriller).
echo-park.Avon Park Terrace Mayan Revival Bungalow: A rare and striking example of Mayan Revival architecture applied at a residential scale, designed by the eccentric architect Robert Stacy-Judd. Built in 1931, the bungalow court features bold geometric patterns and stylized moldings drawn from ancient Central American temples — an exotic aesthetic Stacy-Judd championed as the basis for a genuinely "American" architectural style, distinct from European traditions. Unlike grander Mayan Revival statements such as Stacy-Judd's own Aztec Hotel in Monrovia, these bungalows bring the style to an intimate, everyday residential scale, making them one of the rarest surviving examples of this brief but fascinating chapter in Los Angeles architectural history.
echo-park.Beach Boys / Hite Morgan Studio: Site of the earliest recording sessions for the Beach Boys in the early 1960s.
echo-park.Belmont Tunnel / Toluca Substation: Tucked away behind a modern apartment complex near Echo Park, this graffitied archway and abandoned substation building are the last visible remnants of the Pacific Electric Railway's 1925 subway system that once shuttled red cars directly through the hills of Downtown LA.
echo-park.Canyon Coffee: A stylish neighborhood hub serving their own locally roasted beans in a bright space.
echo-park.Carey McWilliams House: Home of the radical journalist and author of Southern California: An Island on the Land. This house was a hub for the "Red Hill" intelligentsia. While not a "high-design" modernist work in the same vein as Schindler, its historical significance to L.A.'s political identity is immense.
echo-park.Cookbook: A modern cornerstone of the neighborhood's culinary identity, specializing in locally sourced produce and specialty goods.
echo-park.Early Echo Park Art Galleries: A vintage building that has historically housed Early Echo Park Art Galleries.
echo-park.Echoplex: A premier underground music venue hosting a diverse range of indie, electronic, and touring acts.
echo-park.Edendale Mural: Edendale is a vibrant mural by Frank Romero situated under the Hollywood Freeway (101) overpass in Echo Park. Commemorates the early silent film era when the neighborhood was known as the Edendale film colony. Features Romero's signature Chicano-style bold lines, depicting classic cars and film iconography. A favorite for locals, acting as a colorful gateway between Echo Park and Silver Lake.
echo-park.Eightfold Coffee: A minimalist cafe known for high-quality roasts and a serene, airy atmosphere.
echo-park.Estelle Lawton Lindsey House: Lindsey was the first woman to serve on the Los Angeles City Council. Her home represents the early 20th-century development of the Heights, characterized by a more traditional, sturdy construction that preceded the modernist boom.
echo-park.Gold Room: A classic Echo Park dive bar known for its high energy and long-standing drink specials.
echo-park.Guisados: Famous for authentic, homestyle Mexican braises and handmade corn tortillas.
echo-park.Harwell Hamilton Harris House (1935): Harris was a protege of Neutra and Schindler, and his own house is a seminal work of "Regional Modernism." It utilizes wood in a way that feels modern but warmer than the European International Style.
echo-park.House of Spirits: A neighborhood landmark known for its classic Googie-style signage.
echo-park.Jensen's Recreation Center: A 1924 commercial landmark featuring a massive rooftop neon sign; an anchor of Echo Park's historic main street.
echo-park.Klock House: A significant local landmark that contributes to the high-density "hillside vernacular" of Valentine Street, sitting in the shadow of the more famous Ross House.
echo-park.Konbi Ni: A walk-up window legendary for its precise Japanese-style egg salad and katsu sandwiches.
echo-park.Little Joy Cocktails: A low-key neighborhood bar that features DJs and a lively late-night dance floor.
echo-park.Mack Sennett Keystone Studios Site: A foundational site in the history of cinema, this Glendale Boulevard address was the original production hub of Mack Sennett's Keystone Studios during the "Edendale" era of filmmaking in the 1910s. Here the Keystone Cops were born — the chaotic, slapstick police characters whose anarchic shorts defined early silent comedy and made Sennett the "King of Comedy." Most significantly, it is where Charlie Chaplin first appeared on screen in 1914, making his celluloid debut and beginning one of the most consequential careers in the history of art. While the original massive stages no longer stand, a historical marker designates the site for those tracing the Edendale film colony that predated Hollywood as the center of the American film industry.
echo-park.Monty's Good Burger (Echo Park): A retro-style plant-based joint featuring 100% vegan burgers and milkshakes.
echo-park.OTOTO: 2023 Outstanding Wine and Other Beverages Program James Beard Foundation Award Winner. An intimate Echo Park bar offering one of the most educational and diverse sake programs in the country.
echo-park.Paul Landacre House - El Moran (HCM #839: The home and studio of the legendary woodblock artist Paul Landacre. It is a humble, handmade redwood structure perched on a ridge. It represents the "bohemian" layer of Elysian Heights—where the architecture was less about formal modernism and more about providing a quiet, light-filled space for a master craftsman.
echo-park.Peter Shire's Studio: The creative heart of one of Los Angeles' most influential living artists, whose work bridges the boundary between fine art and functional design. A founding member of the legendary Milan-based Memphis Group — the radical 1980s design collective known for its bold colors, geometric postmodernism, and defiant rejection of modernist minimalism — Shire brought that sensibility home to the Echo Park hills. The studio itself is a visual landmark: bold primary colors and playful structural elements announce it from the street. The distinctive Echo Park teapots and sculptures found in museums and collections worldwide were conceived within these walls.
echo-park.Quarter Sheets: 2026 Outstanding Pastry Chef or Baker James Beard Foundation Award Nominee. A buzzy Echo Park bakery and pizzeria celebrated for Hannah Ziskin's innovative cakes and "slabs."
echo-park.Ross House (1938) \u2014 HCM #964:
The Vibe: This is an early, critical example of Soriano’s "Steel Modernism." Built for Albert and Beulah Ross, it sits on a steep Echo Park lot. It uses a light steel frame and massive bands of windows.

Gazetteer Note: This was one of the first homes in L.A. to truly embrace the International Style without the "softness" of the regional wood-centric modernism. It's essentially a high-precision glass box floating over a hillside.

As we noted earlier, this is a foundational Historic-Cultural Monument (#964). It is one of the earliest examples of Soriano’s steel-frame residential work, utilizing light industrial materials to conquer a steep hillside.
echo-park.Sage Regenerative Kitchen: A spacious plant-based eatery and brewery focusing on sustainable comfort food.
echo-park.Salkin House: Mid-century modern residence on Avon Terrace.
echo-park.St Paul Commons Retreat: A serene community and spiritual facility overlooking Echo Park Lake, used for workshops and local retreats.
echo-park.St. Anne\u2019s: A historic social service agency in Silver Lake/Echo Park dedicated to supporting young mothers and families.
echo-park.The Baxter Street Stairs: A pedestrian stairway cutting through one of the steepest residential corridors in the United States — Baxter Street itself carries a harrowing 32% grade. The stairs offer a safer, scenic alternative for residents and hikers navigating the ridge, rewarding the climb with panoramic views spanning the Downtown LA skyline, the Hollywood Sign, and the San Gabriel Mountains. Part of the legendary network of "Secret Stairs" built in the early 20th century to connect hillside residents to the Red Car trolley lines below, the Baxter stairs are a tangible remnant of the pedestrian infrastructure that shaped Echo Park long before the automobile era.
echo-park.The Music Box Steps: Perhaps the most culturally significant public stairway in Los Angeles. These 133 steps on Vendome Street served as the primary location for Laurel and Hardy's 1932 Oscar-winning short film The Music Box, in which the duo famously attempts to haul a piano up the brutal incline — a gag sequence that remains one of the most beloved in comedy history. A commemorative plaque at the base marks the site, which remains largely unchanged since filming. Every year the neighborhood hosts a "Music Box Day" festival celebrating both the film and the stairway's enduring legacy as a piece of living cinematic history.
echo-park.The Philip Dike House (1930s): Designed for the famous California Water Color Society artist Philip Dike. Much like the Landacre house, this residence was built to capture the specific, soft northern light of the Echo Park hills, essential for his landscape painting.
echo-park.The Sayre House (Reservoir Dogs Location: A modest Victorian-style home in the Echo Park hills that gained cult status as a key location in Quentin Tarantino's 1992 debut feature Reservoir Dogs. The house served as the apartment of undercover cop "Mr. Orange" (Tim Roth), and its rooftop and interior were used for the pivotal scenes in which Orange rehearses his elaborate undercover "commode story" — one of the film's most celebrated sequences. For devotees of Tarantino's work and 1990s independent cinema, the house remains a quiet pilgrimage point on the map of Los Angeles film history.
echo-park.The Short Stop: The Short Stop, a legendary dive bar and former LAPD officer hangout near Dodger Stadium.
echo-park.The Southall House (Rudolph Schindler, 1: A masterpiece of Schindler’s "Plaster Skin" period. Designed for Mildred Southall, a music teacher, it features an L-shaped plan with a soaring studio space. The house is famous for its "niche" seating and built-in furniture that blurs the line between the building and its contents.
echo-park.Tom of Finland Foundation: The world's leading cultural repository for the work of Touko Laaksonen, housed in a historic Echo Park residence.
echo-park.Tsubaki: An intimate modern izakaya serving seasonal Japanese small plates and craft sake.

el-monte.10029 Valley Boulevard El Monte, CA 9173: Santa Fe Trail Historical Park, a landmark marking the "End of the Santa Fe Trail" and the site of the first permanent settlement by U.S. citizens in Southern California.
el-monte.10724 E. Lower Azusa Road. El Monte, CA: Driftwood Dairy, a spectacular and rare example of Googie-style "Space Age" drive-thru architecture built in 1961, featuring a massive parabolic arch canopy.

el-monte.3534 Peck Rd, El Monte, CA:
The History: This site is significant for its role in the early 20th-century transit history of Southern California. It was part of the Pacific Electric "Red Car" system, which was once the largest electric railway network in the world.

Modern Era: While the Red Cars are long gone, this corridor remains a transit hub for the San Gabriel Valley, serving as a reminder of how the interurban rail system originally shaped the development and connectivity of El Monte to the rest of the Los Angeles basin.
el-monte.4264 Peck Road El Monte, CA 91732-2114: Jolly Jug  ! - Bizarre and Wonderful Frog Sign.
el-monte.El Monte Historical Museum: Housed in a 1920s-era former library; documents the city's legacy as the final stop for cross-country pioneers on the Santa Fe Trail.
el-monte.Gay's Lion Farm (Ghost Site): The site of a world-famous 1920s-40s attraction where the MGM lions were bred and trained; a legendary piece of SELA/SGV crossover lore.
el-monte.Los Angeles Lion Farm: Operating from 1925 to 1942, this was the "Disneyland" of the 1930s. It housed over 200 African lions used primarily for the film industry, including the original MGM roaring lions, "Slats" and "Jackie." It was a massive tourist draw where visitors could watch trainers interact with the cats at close range.
el-monte.Norms Restaurant: This is a Norms Restaurant opened in 2018 that features "Retro Nuevo Googie" architecture—a modern take on the classic space-age style—complete with the chain's iconic orange sawtooth sign and expansive glass windows designed to catch the eye of passing motorists.

Historically a vital watering hole on the old Stockton–Los Angeles Road during the Gold Rush, it remains a serene residential enclave surrounded by the Angeles National Forest.

elysian-park.1051 North Broadway Los Angeles, CA 9001: Nick’s Cafe, a historic 1948 horseshoe-counter diner famous for its "ham house" breakfast.
elysian-park.Chavez Ravine (Historic): Carved into the hills of Chavez Ravine in 1962, this mid-century modern ballpark is the third-oldest in Major League Baseball. It is renowned for its iconic pastel color palette and sweeping, unobstructed views of the San Gabriel Mountains and the downtown skyline.
elysian-park.Chavez Ravine Arboretum: Established in 1893, the Chavez Ravine Arboretum is the oldest arboretum in Southern California. Tucked within Elysian Park, its collection of historic specimen trees—including many rare and champion-sized examples—represents over 130 years of botanical history in the heart of Los Angeles.
elysian-park.Dodger Stadium: Built in 1962, Dodger Stadium is the home of the Los Angeles Dodgers and the third-oldest ballpark in Major League Baseball. It is set dramatically into the hillside of Chávez Ravine, offering sweeping views of the Los Angeles skyline and the San Gabriel Mountains.
elysian-park.Sunken Ruins of Chavez Ravine: Before Dodger Stadium was built, this area was home to the vibrant communities of La Loma, Palo Verde, and Bishop. After the city used eminent domain to clear the land for a failed housing project, the residents were forcibly removed. Today, if you hike the ridges behind the stadium parking lot, you can still find old concrete foundations and residential staircases leading to nowhere.

encino.17958 Ventura Boulevard Encino, CA 91316:
The Context: Located on a prominent stretch of Ventura Boulevard, this area represents the commercial heart of the San Fernando Valley's south side.

Key Features: This specific commercial block has historically housed local mainstays and professional services that cater to the affluent residential hills above. It is a quintessential example of the "Boulevard culture" that defines Valley life—where upscale retail, classic delis, and modern wellness centers sit side-by-side along the region’s most famous thoroughfare.
encino.4111 Valley Meadow Road in Encino's Roya:
The History: Located in the prestigious, guard-gated Royal Oaks community, this address is part of an enclave long favored by celebrities seeking privacy and large, flat lots in the hills.

The Legacy: This specific property was famously owned by John Travolta and Kelly Preston for over 20 years. They purchased the nearly 3-acre estate in the late 90s and it served as their primary LA residence for decades.

Key Architectural Features: The estate features a massive 10,000+ square foot main house, a detached guest house, and park-like grounds with mature trees that emphasize the secluded, "country in the city" feel that makes Royal Oaks the most coveted pocket of Encino.
encino.Cher's House (Clueless): The massive mansion used as the exterior of Cher Horowitz's home in the quintessential 90s teen comedy.
encino.Encino Community Center: A central park facility in the San Fernando Valley offering a wide range of senior services and youth activities.
encino.Encino Oak Tree (Site of): The site of the great live oak tree of Encino, estimated to be over 1,000 years old when it fatally collapsed in 1998. The tree was a regional landmark and gathering place that predated Spanish settlement by centuries, and its loss was mourned throughout the San Fernando Valley.
encino.Encino Park Liquor (Neon Sign): Local liquor store notable for its classic neon sign.
encino.Entourage House (Johnny Drama / Vince): The Tuscan-style mansion used early in the series as the home base for Vincent Chase and his entourage.
encino.La La Land Pool Party Filming Location: Residential property used for the pool party scene in the 2016 film La La Land.
encino.La La Land Someone in the Crowd House: The private residence used for the iconic, color-drenched pool party sequence in the 2016 film La La Land.
encino.Los Encinos State Historic Park: The site of a former ranch that served as a critical watering hole for travelers on El Camino Real, featuring original adobe structures.
encino.Old Nike Station LA-96C Radar Tower (Ben: Abandoned Nike missile radar station hidden in the Santa Monica Mountains above Encino.
encino.Rahad Jackson's House (Boogie Nights): The site of Boogie Nights' famously tense, botched drug deal involving firecrackers and the song Jessie's Girl. While the original 1964 home used for filming was completely remodeled in 2005, the address remains an infamous piece of cinematic history.
encino.San Vicente Mountain Park (Nike Missile: Standing high above the Encino reservoir, this former Cold War anti-aircraft missile control site still features an imposing, rusty radar tower and concrete bunkers that visitors can climb for sweeping, 360-degree views of the sprawling city below.

los-angeles-exposition-park.Historic Palm Tree of Los Angeles: This historic palm tree stood for more than 25 years at the entrance of the Southern Pacific Station. At this railroad portal of the city it became a familiar landmark to many thousands of Los Angeles citizens. When construction of a new station necessitated its removal, it was presented to the City of Los Angeles by the Southern Pacific Company. On September 5, 1914, it was placed here, where it and its sentimental associations will be permanently preserved.
