lancaster.The Western Hotel Museum: The Western Hotel Museum, built in 1888, is the oldest surviving building in Lancaster and a designated California Historic Landmark.

larchmont-village.Whatever Happened to Baby Jane House: The 1962 classic film starring Bette Davis and Joan Crawford used this grand residence as the exterior for the sisters home.

lawndale.16418 Hawthorne Boulevard Lawndale, CA 9: Established furniture showroom with a wide array of products, including mattresses & accent pieces.
lawndale.Alondra Park: A substantial 50-acre historic green space developed during the post-WWII housing boom, featuring extensive community recreation facilities, a large man-made lake, and a native-plant island.
lawndale.Frank's Furniture: Frank's Furniture, a family-owned business that has operated from this same South Bay location for over half a century.

leimert-park._neighborhood: The cultural hub of African American art and jazz in LA.
leimert-park.Egyptian Court: A fraternal organization hall on Crenshaw Boulevard, part of the civic and social fabric of the Leimert Park community. The Egyptian Court building reflects the Egyptian Revival architectural motif that enjoyed popularity in the early 20th century following the 1922 discovery of Tutankhamun's tomb, a style that found particular expression in Southern California's fraternal lodge architecture.
leimert-park.Leimert Plaza Park: A historic public square that serves as a gathering point for drum circles, community festivals, and social activism.
leimert-park.Maverick's Flat: A legendary nightclub known as the "Apollo of the West," central to the soul and jazz scene in the 1960s and 70s.
leimert-park.The Vision Theatre: A 1931 movie palace turned performing arts center; featuring a signature Art Deco tower that serves as the neighborhood's primary visual landmark.
leimert-park.The World Stage: An educational and performance gallery founded by Billy Higgins; a foundational site for the development of West Coast jazz and oral traditions.

lennox.\"Make Love Not War\" Mural: Painted by a local artist and CSUN student Laura Medrano in 2021, this vibrant community mural features the phrase 'Make Love Not War' alongside the depiction of a couple, a sun, and roses. It stands as a proud local landmark reflecting the cultural identity and community pride of the Lennox neighborhood.
lennox.The Lennox Car Wash: A classic example of the early 1960s post-war, Googie car-oriented commercial architecture that once defined Southern California. The Lennox Car Wash is characterized by sweeping geometric shapes, vibrant neon signage, and dramatic, spear-like fins pointing to the sky. The visually arresting structure was featured in the 2011 film The Green Hornet and was meticulously recreated in the video game Grand Theft Auto V as the 'Hands On Car Wash'.

lincoln-heights.1145 NMission Rd Los Angeles, CA 90031: The main facility for the Los Angeles County Department of Medical Examiner-Coroner.
lincoln-heights.145 WAvenue 26, Los Angeles, CA 90031-18: An industrial facility in Lincoln Heights utilized for manufacturing and creative studios.
lincoln-heights.2740 Prewett St \u2014 Amazing View: A Lincoln Heights hillside address with remarkable panoramic views of the surrounding neighborhood and cityscape.
lincoln-heights.Big Art Labs: Artist studio and fabrication space on Clover St.
lincoln-heights.Broadway Social Center Pool Hall: Local pool hall and social gathering spot on N Broadway.
lincoln-heights.California Alligator Farm: From 1907 to 1953, this farm allowed guests to handle reptiles, watch them slide down chutes, and even ride on the backs of larger alligators. It provided the gators for many early Tarzan films before moving to Buena Park in the 1950s.
lincoln-heights.Charles Lummis Residence (El Alisal): Built by hand by journalist, activist, and preservationist Charles Fletcher Lummis using stones from the Arroyo Seco, El Alisal ('The Sycamore') was the center of Arroyo Culture—a defining intellectual and artistic movement of turn-of-the-century Los Angeles. Now the headquarters of the Historical Society of Southern California.
lincoln-heights.Chinatown Church of Christ: A significant religious landmark that reflects the historic intersection of the Chinese and Latino communities in Lincoln Heights.
lincoln-heights.Church of the Epiphany: The oldest Episcopal church in Los Angeles, it served as a central organizing hub for the Chicano Movement in the 1960s.
lincoln-heights.Combined Forces Mural (Paul Botello): A massive, powerful mural by renowned artist Paul Botello, representing themes of community, strength, and local identity.
lincoln-heights.El Huarachito: A legendary breakfast spot rated as one of the best in the city, featuring murals and a menu rooted in deep local tradition.
lincoln-heights.Federal Bank Building (HCM #396): A 1910 Neoclassical bank building designed by Neher and Skilling, currently occupied by El Pollo Loco.
lincoln-heights.HM157 (HCM #157): A historic 1886 Victorian residence that now serves as an underground cultural hub, art space, and venue for folk music.
lincoln-heights.La Mano Press: Independent letterpress print shop and community arts space.
lincoln-heights.Lincoln Heights Historic Preservation Ov:
The History: Recognized as a Historic Preservation Overlay Zone, Lincoln Heights holds the distinction of being considered Los Angeles's first true suburb, originally subdivided in the 1870s.

Neighborhood Character: The HPOZ protects a dense and diverse collection of late 19th and early 20th-century residential architecture, including stunning Queen Anne, Craftsman, and Folk Victorian homes. It remains a deeply historic, working-class neighborhood that reflects the layered, multicultural history of the city's Eastside.
lincoln-heights.Lincoln Heights Jail: A monolithic, abandoned Art Deco prison that has sat largely vacant for decades; its imposing concrete shell is a popular 'ghostly' backdrop for major film and music video productions.
lincoln-heights.Lincoln Heights Recreation Center: A historic park facility in one of L.A.'s oldest neighborhoods, providing youth sports and public events.
lincoln-heights.Lincoln Heights Urban Settlement: Historic urban settlement area in Lincoln Heights on Johnston St.
lincoln-heights.Los Angeles Ostrich Farm: Located right next door to the Alligator Farm, this was the direct competitor to South Pasadena's Cawston Farm. It offered similar ostrich-drawn carriage rides and photo opportunities with the giant birds, capitalizing on the "animal row" that developed along Mission Road.
lincoln-heights.Mi Barra Deportes Bar: Local sports bar on N San Fernando Rd.
lincoln-heights.Palms Southern Pacific Railroad Depot (H: A late 19th-century residence and garden preserved at Heritage Square Museum, reflecting the horticultural ambitions of early Los Angeles homemakers in the period before the automobile transformed the landscape.
lincoln-heights.Ptomaine Tommy's (Historic Restaurant Si: Site of Ptomaine Tommy's, one of LA's earliest and most famous chili parlors, operating from the 1910s through the 1950s.
lincoln-heights.San Antonio Winery: Founded in 1917, San Antonio is the last surviving commercial winery established in Los Angeles during the pre-Prohibition era. It survived by obtaining a sacramental wine exemption and has operated continuously ever since, a remarkable industrial and cultural survivor in Lincoln Heights.
lincoln-heights.Selig Zoo and Movie Studio: Founded by film pioneer William "Colonel" Selig, this was a massive 32-acre zoo and movie studio combo. It featured an elaborate arched entrance with lavish animal sculptures. The zoo provided animals for Selig’s "jungle" pictures and eventually evolved into the Luna Park Zoo before closing around 1940.

little-bangladesh.Meghna Restaurant: Bangladeshi restaurant on W 3rd St in the Little Bangladesh corridor.

lomita.2347 Pacific Coast Hwy Lomita, CA 90717: The Hot N Tot, a beloved family-style diner and local institution that has served Lomita for nearly 40 years.
lomita.Lomita Railroad Museum: A dedicated monument to the steam era, founded in 1966. It serves as a visual and historical anchor for the city's early 20th-century roots.
lomita.The Lamplighter Apartments: A classic 1960s Dingbat complex featuring the era's signature stylized script signage and boxy, stilt-supported architecture.

long-beach.2400 East 7th Street Long Beach, CA 9080: Haskell's Prospector Steak House and Saloon - from hell-o-sunshine.blogspot.com_.

This local landmark was a filming site for the TV series Dexter, appearing during the 'Driving with Harry' sequence.

long-beach.The Sovereign:
Architectural Style: Beaux-Arts

The History: This twelve-story historic landmark was originally built as a luxury hotel and apartment building during Long Beach's early 20th-century coastal boom.

Key Features: The building is famous for its intricate terracotta ornamentation and its "Sovereign" neon roof sign, which has served as a navigational beacon for the Long Beach shoreline for over a century.

los-feliz.The Castle (A.F. Leicht Mansion): Located in Los Feliz just up the street from the famous Ennis House, this 1924 Art Nouveau masterpiece was designed by eccentric Los Angeles architect A.F. Leicht. It features sprawling stone turrets, massive arched windows, octagonal rooms, and sweeping staircases, looking less like a traditional Hollywood Hills mansion and more like a towering Scottish fortress.
los-feliz.The Dresden Room: Walking into the Dresden (est. 1954) is like stepping onto the set of the movie Swingers. The white vinyl booths and the iconic lounge—where the legendary Marty and Elayne performed for decades—capture the lounge-act cool of the 1950s and 60s.
los-feliz.The Ennis House (LA's Machu Picchu): Frank Lloyd Wright's 1924 textile-block masterpiece, nicknamed 'LA's Machu Picchu' for its monumental Mayan Revival facade. Featured in Blade Runner and other films.
los-feliz.The Los Feliz Murder Mansion (Perelson H: A beautiful Spanish Colonial Revival home that sat abandoned and frozen in time for decades following a 1959 murder-suicide. For over 50 years, locals could peer through the windows and see the original Christmas tree and wrapped presents still sitting in the living room. It was finally sold and renovated in recent years, but it remains a staple of LA urban legend.
los-feliz.The Prospect Studios: Historic film and TV production studio complex.
los-feliz.The Vista Theatre: Located where Hollywood meets Los Feliz, this 1923 single-screen theater is famous for its elaborate Egyptian Revival interior and classic, flashing neon marquee. True to its vintage roots, the theater has a strict policy of projecting films exclusively on real 35mm and 70mm film stock. The forecourt features handprints and footprints of independent and cult filmmakers, serving as an alternative to the mainstream Grauman's Chinese Theatre.
los-feliz.Trails Cafe: Popular hiking cafe at the entrance to Griffith Park on Fern Dell Dr.

manhattan-beach.Bruce's Beach: A site of immense historical significance as the first Black-owned resort in LA (1912). In 1924, the city used eminent domain to seize the land. In 2021, the property was historicially returned to the Bruce family descendants.
manhattan-beach.Metlox Plaza (Former Metlox Potteries): A 1927-1989 industrial hub. Once one of the 'Big Five' California potteries, it marks Manhattan Beach's blue-collar past before its transformation into a luxury retail center.
manhattan-beach.The 'Shore Acres' Ghost District: The northern section of the city, which nearly became its namesake. Developer George Peck lost a coin toss in 1902 to Stewart Merrill, who insisted on 'Manhattan' after his New York home.
manhattan-beach.The Kettle: Since 1973, this has been the "town square" of Manhattan Beach. It is a 24-hour haven that feels like a cozy, wood-paneled transition between the beach culture of the 60s and the suburbia of the 70s.
manhattan-beach.The Sand Wars Excavation Site: The site of the massive dunes that once reached 50 feet. During the 1920s, the sand was extracted and shipped to build Downtown LA and to nourish the beaches of Waikiki, Hawaii.
manhattan-beach.Velzy & Jacobs Surfboards (World's First: Opened by Dale Velzy in 1950, this was the world's first retail surfboard shop, making Manhattan Beach the epicenter of the early commercial surf industry.

mar-vista.Gregory Ain Mar Vista Tract HPOZ: A groundbreaking 1948 post-war housing tract designed by architect Gregory Ain. It stands as a premier example of mid-century modern 'social architecture,' featuring innovatively staggered lots and seamlessly integrated indoor-outdoor living spaces.

melrose-district.Fred Segal (Original Site): The longtime ivy-covered corner that defined the 'California Cool' retail experience from the 1960s through the early 2000s.
melrose-district.Melrose Place: A secluded, high-end sub-district featuring lush courtyards and ivy-covered storefronts that contrast with the gritty, neon aesthetic of the main boulevard.
melrose-district.Tail o' the Pup (Original Site): The gold standard of 'L.A. Kitsch'; a hot-dog-shaped building that represents the city's 20th-century tradition of buildings shaped like the products they sell.
melrose-district.The Paul Smith 'Pink Wall': A monochromatic pink facade that has become a global landmark of 'Instagram architecture,' demonstrating the power of singular color in urban design.

mid.1101 S La Brea Ave: A Union 76 Station featuring a distinctive mid-century "Googie" cantilevered roof design.
mid.1255 Westchester Place: A contributing property within the Country Club Park historic district, situated along a street known for its diverse and grand residential architecture ranging from Craftsman to Mediterranean Revival.
An independent roastery representing the modern creative class and small-business revitalization along the Washington Blvd corridor.
mid.Brookledge Theater (Precursor to the Mag: Located in the Brookside area of Mid-City, the Brookledge Theater at 929 S. Longwood was an intimate private magic theater founded by Milt Larsen, which directly inspired and preceded the creation of the Magic Castle in Hollywood.
mid.Brother Bar-B-Que: A traditional BBQ institution that has anchored the Crenshaw/Mid-City culinary scene for decades.
mid.Dingbat Apartment Building: A classic example of Dingbat architecture.
mid.Dunsmuir Flats:
Architect: Gregory Ain

Architectural Style: International Style

The History: Designated as Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument #954, this complex is a premier example of Gregory Ain's commitment to providing high-quality, modern, affordable housing.

Key Architectural Features: The site consists of four interconnected units stepped along the slope to ensure maximum privacy for each balcony and garden. It is characterized by smooth white stucco walls and continuous ribbon windows that flood the units with natural light.
mid.Honey Bee's House of Breakfast: A classic neighborhood staple known for its home-style breakfast and deep ties to the West Adams community.
mid.Jewel's Catch One: Jewel's Catch One in Arlington Heights was the city's first Black gay and lesbian disco, operating since 1972.
mid.Paul R. Williams Home: The personal residence of the 'Architect to the Stars'; a landmark of Black architectural achievement in L.A.
mid.Ralph Vaughn House at 1212 Westchester P: Architect: Ralph Vaughn. Significance: This site is significant for its association with Ralph Vaughn, one of Los Angeles' most influential early African-American architects. Vaughn was a pioneer in the design of large-scale, high-density housing complexes and worked on numerous significant projects across the city, including the Lincoln Heights and Venice areas.
mid.Roscoe's House of Chicken and Waffles: A legendary Southern California institution and a central social anchor for the region's diverse creative community.
mid.St. Elmo Art Collective: A 1969-founded art sanctuary and bungalow colony; a landmark of community-driven creative preservation.
mid.The Ebell of Los Angeles: A 1927 historic club and theater; an anchor of Wilshire Boulevard's civic architecture.
mid.The Marvin Gaye House: A massive, once-stately mansion where the legendary soul singer was tragically shot and killed by his own father in 1984. The house has since fallen into various states of repair, standing as a quiet, residential monument to one of the most tragic days in music history.
mid.The Murder House \u2014 American Horror: Known in real life as the Rosenheim Mansion, this looming property in the Arlington Heights neighborhood was the centerpiece of the first season of American Horror Story. It has a notoriously spooky reputation both on and off the screen.
mid.The Rosenheim Mansion (Murder House): Architect: Alfred F. Rosenheim (built 1908). A three-story, 15,000-square-foot Collegiate Gothic-style mansion. It is widely recognized as the "Murder House" from the first season of American Horror Story. The estate features a private chapel, a recording studio (formerly a pilot’s lounge), and Tiffany stained-glass windows.
mid.Vineyard Recreation Center: A local Mid-City park and facility offering basketball, youth programs, and a community meeting room.
mid.Washington Irving Branch Library: A key civic and historical repository serving the Mid-City and West Adams neighborhoods since the mid-20th century.

mid-city-west.Dingbat Apartment (S Holt Ave): Classic example of the Los Angeles 'dingbat' stucco apartment typology.

mid-city-west.Heinsbergen Decorating Company Building: Home of the Heinsbergen Decorating Company, whose ornate painted interiors graced countless movie palaces and landmark buildings across LA.
mid-city-west.Lee's Liquors: Local liquor store on W 3rd St.
mid-city-west.Melrose Automatic Laundry: Local laundromat on N La Brea Ave.
mid-city-west.Memorial Branch Library: A significant branch of the Los Angeles Public Library system serving the Mid-City/Olympic Park corridor, representing the city's commitment to public literacy and education in working-class and immigrant communities.
mid-city-west.Patsy D'Amore's Pizza: Legendary Italian pizza institution, once the preferred pie of Frank Sinatra and the Rat Pack.
mid-city-west.Paul R. Williams Residence: The long-time home of pioneering architect Paul R. Williams, who designed homes for many of Hollywood's elite.
mid-city-west.Rocket Video: Beloved independent video rental store on N La Brea Ave.
mid-city-west.William Grant Still Residence: The historic home of the 'Dean of Afro-American Composers,' William Grant Still.
mid-city-west.Wilshire Ward Chapel: A late-modernist spiritual site that reflects the mid-century architectural evolution of the Olympic Park area.

miracle-mile.Dominguez-Wilshire Building: A 1931 Morgan, Walls & Clements landmark; known for its high-fashion history and intricate vertical terra-cotta detailing.
miracle-mile.E. Clem Wilson Building: An Art Deco gem once featuring a Gothic crown and a dirigible mast; the former eastern anchor of the district.
miracle-mile.El Rey Theatre: A 1936 Clifford Balch-designed theater with a signature neon marquee and perfectly preserved lobby aesthetics.
miracle-mile.Fox Apartments: Historic apartment building on S Burnside Ave.
miracle-mile.Johnie's Coffee Shop: A 1956 filming icon; designated as a Historic-Cultural Monument to preserve its role as a quintessential L.A. movie backdrop with its striking 'butterfly roof' Googie design.

monterey-park.\u201cEl Paradiso\u201d (1964) \u2014 HC:
The Vibe: This is a late-career masterpiece for Soriano. "El Paradiso" (The Paradise) is a rare example of a modernist hilltop villa in Monterey Park. It utilizes a radical, wide-span steel structure that allows for virtually no interior load-bearing walls.

Gazetteer Note: It represents the peak of Soriano’s obsession with prefabrication. The house was designed to be easily replicated, though it remains a one-of-a-kind monument to his modular philosophy.

north-hollywood.Allee Willis House: This is the Allee Willis House (also known as "Willis Wonderland"). Built in 1937, it is a rare and pristine example of Streamline Moderne architecture designed by William Kesling. It was famously the home of the late Allee Willis, the Grammy-winning songwriter who wrote the Friends theme song and hits for Earth, Wind & Fire. An original portfolio piece by architect William Kesling, this Streamline Moderne residence is a rare example of Los Angeles’ celebrated “ocean liner” style. Ideally situated near major studios, the property blends architectural pedigree with a storied Hollywood past. In 1980, the home was purchased by Grammy-winning songwriter and art director Allee Willis, who restored its original character while giving it the signature pink exterior it’s known for today. Willis used the residence as both a private creative retreat and a venue for her famed gatherings, welcoming collaborators and industry icons from Cher to Bob Dylan. Set on lush grounds beneath towering palms, the property offers playful mid-century design elements including a bowling-ball garden, private sand beach, and a newly built poolside shower. Select furnishings and collectibles may be available, offering buyers a unique opportunity to acquire not only a historic residence but also a piece of Hollywood’s cultural legacy.
north-hollywood.Archie's Ranch Market: A vintage market site with notable mid-century signage.
north-hollywood.Barris Kustom Industries: The workshop of George Barris, creator of the Batmobile; the undisputed center of global Kustom design history. Located in North Hollywood, this was the "throne" of the King of Kustomizers, George Barris. Barris and his brother Sam essentially pioneered the "Kustom" with a "K" branding, signifying a more radical, high-design approach to modification. The Hollywood Connection: Barris was the go-to builder for the studios, creating the original 1966 Batmobile, the Munster Koach, and KITT from Knight Rider. Design Innovations: The shop perfected the "Hirohata Merc" style—radically chopping rooflines and "sectioning" bodies to create sleek, low silhouettes that Detroit would later imitate. Cultural Preservation: While George passed in 2015, the shop remains an active landmark of custom car history, still modding cars and serving as a pilgrimage site for "gearheads" worldwide.

north-hollywood.The Palomino Club (Former Site):
The History: From 1949 until it closed in 1995, the Palomino Club was arguably the most important country music club on the West Coast. Founded by Hank Penny and Amand Gautier, it became a legendary honky-tonk that hosted the biggest names in the genre, including Johnny Cash, Willie Nelson, Patsy Cline, and Linda Ronstadt.

Cultural Legacy: In the 1980s, the venue became the epicenter of the "Cow Punk" movement, breaking acts like Lone Justice and Dwight Yoakam. Because of its authentic, rough-and-tumble atmosphere, it was heavily favored by Hollywood stuntmen and served as a filming location for Clint Eastwood's Every Which Way But Loose. While the building is now a banquet hall, its iconic neon sign was saved and is displayed at the Valley Relics Museum.

panorama-city.The Panorama Theatre: The Panorama Theatre, a historic 1949 movie palace designed by the legendary architect William L. Pereira (famous for the Transamerica Pyramid and LAX). The building is a designated landmark featuring a striking vertical neon tower; a time capsule containing 1940s memorabilia like bobby socks and photos of Frank Sinatra is reportedly buried in its foundation.
panorama-city.The Panorama Towers (Mad Men): A mid-century high-rise office building designed by Welton Becket that appeared in numerous productions, including Mad Men, for its period-accurate 1960s aesthetic.
panorama-city.Van Nuys Blvd Cruising Stretch: This location sits along the historic "Van Nuys Blvd Cruising" stretch. During the 1960s and 70s, this specific corridor was the epicenter of Southern California car culture, where thousands would gather to show off custom "wheels."
panorama-city.Van Nuys Boulevard (Boulevard Nights): The stretch of the boulevard through Panorama City was a focal point for the 1979 film Boulevard Nights, capturing authentic local lowrider and cruising culture.

Features beautiful, ornate streetlamps and dramatic, sweeping arches that look incredibly cinematic at sunset. Has a long history in film and television, most notably serving as the romantic backdrop for an evening stroll in La La Land (2016).
pasadena.Don & Betty Draper's House (Mad Men): The beautiful, manicured colonial residence used as the Draper family's Ossining, New York home in Mad Men.
pasadena.Eaton Canyon Foothills Residential Area: Located in the Eaton Canyon foothills, this residential area is known for its rugged natural beauty and proximity to the city’s major hiking and nature preserves.
pasadena.Historic Old Pasadena Alleyway (Lost Par: Located in Old Pasadena, this historic brick-lined alleyway is a survivor of the city’s original commercial core. It represents the successful urban revitalization of the district and currently houses local favorites like Lost Parrot Cafe.
pasadena.Historic Old Pasadena Commercial Archite: Situated in the heart of Old Pasadena, this site was historically part of the Pasadena Star-News complex. It is a key example of the historic brick commercial architecture that defines the city's downtown shopping district.
pasadena.La Miniatura (Millard House) \u2014 Fran: Completed in 1924 for rare-book dealer Alice Millard, La Miniatura is the first and most celebrated of Frank Lloyd Wright's four Los Angeles-area concrete textile-block houses — the others being the Storer House, the Freeman House, and the Ennis House. Nestled in a eucalyptus-shaded ravine in Pasadena, the house takes its nickname from its relatively modest scale, which belies the intricate geometric patterning of its hand-cast concrete blocks and the dramatic way it engages the landscape. Wright considered it one of his finest achievements, writing that it proved a beautiful house could be built for modest means. It is listed on the National Register of Historic Places and remains privately owned.
pasadena.Lee\u2019s Hoagie House: Local establishment on E Colorado Blvd.
pasadena.Odyssey Games Pasadena: Local hobby and game shop on E Colorado Blvd.
pasadena.Old Pasadena: The city's original commercial center, successfully revitalized into a bustling historic district.
pasadena.Pasadena City Hall: Pasadena City Hall, a massive 1927 Beaux-Arts landmark with a 206-foot dome. It is one of the most photographed buildings in the country and stands in for "Pawnee City Hall" in Parks and Recreation.
pasadena.Puelicher House: A striking 1960 modern residence designed by Boyd Georgi.
pasadena.Roma Market: Roma Market, a world-famous Italian grocery and deli. It is the home of the legendary "The Sandwich," created by Rosario Mazzeo, which has remained unchanged for decades and is considered a quintessential Pasadena culinary experience.
pasadena.The campus of Westridge School: The campus of Westridge School, an elite independent school for girls founded in 1913. The campus features several significant buildings, including works designed by the renowned architectural firm Marston, Van Pelt & Maybury.
pasadena.The Colorado Street Bridge: The Colorado Street Bridge, a 1913 concrete arch bridge. Known as the "Suicide Bridge" for its dark history, it is a stunning landmark seen in Charlie Chaplin's The Kid and the "Lovely Night" stroll in La La Land.
pasadena.The Father of the Bride House: The Father of the Bride House, the quintessential colonial-style home used in the 1991 film; across the street is the Draper Residence from Mad Men.

pasadena.The historic 1934 Santa Fe Depot (Del Ma: The historic 1934 Santa Fe Depot (Del Mar Station). This stunning Spanish Colonial Revival building originally served as the primary gateway for travelers arriving in Pasadena by rail. Today, it is meticulously preserved and houses The Luggage Room Pizzeria and La Grande Orange Cafe.
pasadena.The Huntington Medical Research Institut: The Huntington Medical Research Institutes (HMRI), a globally recognized non-profit biomedical research organization. Since its founding in 1952, it has been a pioneer in medical imaging and neurological research.
pasadena.The Kubly House: The Kubly House, designed by the legendary modernist architect Craig Ellwood in 1965. This residence is a premier example of the International Style, characterized by its glass-and-steel "bridge" structure that appears to float over the natural arroyo landscape.
pasadena.The Pasadena Playhouse: The Pasadena Playhouse, designated as the State Theatre of California. Built in 1925 in the Spanish Colonial Revival style by architect Elmer Grey, it remains one of the most significant regional theaters in the United States and a cornerstone of the city’s cultural history.

pico-rivera.America's Most Wanted Menudo:
This building is a quintessential example of Programmatic (or Mimetic) Architecture, a style that flourished in Southern California during the mid-20th century. The philosophy behind this movement was simple: the building itself should serve as its own advertisement. In an era when drivers were beginning to move at higher speeds, a giant object—like a hat, a bucket, or a take-out container—was more effective than a traditional sign at catching the eye of a motorist.

Design and History: Constructed around 1970, the building was originally a "Chinese Food to Go" stand. Its design features the iconic folded-top shape of a cardboard oyster pail (the classic Chinese take-out box), complete with a giant metal "handle" that arches over the roof.

Historical Context: It belongs to the same architectural lineage as the famous Tail o' the Pup (a hot dog-shaped stand) or The Brown Derby. While many of these structures were demolished as land values rose, the Pico Rivera container has remained a resilient landmark. It has housed various businesses over the decades, including a burger joint and a doughnut shop, but its form remains an enduring tribute to the playful, kitschy roadside architecture of the San Gabriel Valley.
pico-rivera.Dal Rae: A soaring masterpiece of 1958 "Mad Men" style. This is where you go for tableside Caesar salad and Chateaubriand in a room filled with deep leather booths and live piano music. It is a rare, pristine example of the high-end suburban steakhouse from LA’s atomic age.
pico-rivera.Dal Rae Restaurant: Home to the Dal Rae Restaurant, a world-famous fine-dining institution that has remained largely unchanged since it opened in 1958. It is one of the few remaining "old school" Los Angeles restaurants where 1950s classics like Lobster Thermidor, Chateaubriand, and Pepper Steak are still prepared tableside in a mid-century atmosphere featuring high-backed leather booths and a classic piano bar.
pico-rivera.Historic Post-War Garden Apartments: A large-scale multi-family residential landmark built in 1961. This 41-unit complex is a prime example of the post-war "garden apartment" style that was developed to accommodate the massive population boom in the Gateway Cities during the aerospace era.
pico-rivera.Historic Roadside Motel (Motel 6 Pico Ri: This address is currently the Motel 6 Pico Rivera (formerly the Rosemead Motel). It represents the classic roadside hospitality architecture that lined the major thoroughfares connecting Los Angeles to the burgeoning suburbs in the mid-20th century.
pico-rivera.Jay Bharat Catering (Near Former Swap Me: Home to Jay Bharat Catering, a highly regarded institution specializing in diverse Indian cuisines (Gujarati, Punjabi, and Indo-Chinese). This location is also near the former site of the Pico Rivera Indoor Swap Meet, which was a major cultural and commercial hub for decades before being redeveloped into modern industrial space.
pico-rivera.Mid-Century Neighborhood (Near El Rancho: A residential property located in a quintessential mid-century neighborhood near El Rancho High School, characterized by the "Minimal Traditional" and "California Ranch" architecture of the late 1940s and 50s.
pico-rivera.Mr. Ed Liquor: The site of Mr. Ed Liquor, a recognizable local landmark storefront that has served the central Rosemead corridor for several decades.
pico-rivera.Pico Rivera Library: The Pico Rivera Library, a modern 16,000-square-foot facility dedicated in 2013. The building is LEED-certified for sustainable design and replaced the city’s original circular library from 1961; it serves as a primary cultural and educational hub for the region.
pico-rivera.Pico Rivera Sports Arena: A premier venue for Mexican rodeos (charreadas) and concerts; a vital architectural and social icon of Southern California's Latino heritage.
pico-rivera.P\u00edo Pico State Historic Park: Known as "El Ranchito," this is the restored adobe home of Pío Pico, the last Governor of Alta California under Mexican rule. It is one of the few remaining examples of a transition between Mexican and American architectural styles. (Pio Pico State Historic Park)
pico-rivera.Se\u00f1or G's & B: The location of Señor G’s & B, a beloved local "hidden gem" known for its authentic Mexican and American diner-style breakfast and lunch. It is a long-standing community staple recognized for its massive burritos and family-friendly atmosphere.
pico-rivera.Whitwood-Pico Crossing (Ghost Site): A significant geographic intersection marking the boundary lines of the original 19th-century Spanish land grants.

playa-del-rey.Surfridge (Abandoned Neighborhood): Once an elite 1920s enclave of Spanish-style mansions favored by Hollywood celebrities like Cecil B. DeMille, Surfridge was slowly consumed by the expansion of LAX and the unbearable noise of jet engines. By the 1970s, all homes had been demolished or relocated. Today it is a 'ghost neighborhood' where paved streets, curbs, and original streetlights remain intact, but no houses stand. The area is now a protected habitat for the El Segundo Blue Butterfly and is strictly off-limits to the public.

ridge-route._neighborhood: An engineering marvel completed in 1915, this was the first paved highway to bridge the gap between Los Angeles and the Central Valley. san-pedro.Korean Bell of Friendship: Hidden on a coastal cliffside, this massive, abandoned military battery was built in 1914 to defend the Los Angeles harbor. san-pedro.Korean Friendship Bell: This massive 17-ton bronze bell was presented by South Korea to the United States in 1976 to celebrate the U.S. Bicentennial and symbolize friendship between the two nations. It is modeled after the Divine Bell of King Seongdeok and is housed in a traditional pagoda-style pavilion known as the Belfry of Friendship, which features a roof supported by twelve columns representing the Korean zodiac.
san-pedro.Little Italy (San Pedro): Location: Downtown San Pedro (around 6th and 7th Streets).\n\nStatus: Official. San Pedro is home to the largest enclave of Italian Americans in Los Angeles. The "Little Italy" designation was officially recognized recently (2021–2022), and it features the Piazza Miramare and numerous historic Italian social clubs and delis.
san-pedro.Los Angeles Yacht Club: A historic maritime institution established in 1901, anchoring the nautical and sailing community of the Port of Los Angeles.
san-pedro.Marine Mammal Care Center: This non-profit hospital rescues and rehabilitates sick and injured seals and sea lions found along the Los Angeles County coastline. Visitors can observe the recovery of "finned patients," such as elephant seals and California sea lions, in outdoor pools and explore educational exhibits about marine mammal conservation.
san-pedro.Maritime Museum (Ferry Building): The historic transit hub for the waterfront workforce; preserving the maritime labor history of the 1940s.
san-pedro.Peck Park Auditorium: A hillside community hall offering dramatic views and a venue for neighborhood council meetings and youth classes.
san-pedro.Personal Involvement Center: A social services hub in South Los Angeles focused on family support, youth development, and community empowerment.
san-pedro.Point Fermin Landslide Site: The epicenter of the 1929 landslide where the ground dropped up to 11 inches per day, creating the current post-apocalyptic terrain.
san-pedro.Point Fermin Lighthouse: Built in 1874, this Victorian Stick Style lighthouse was the first navigational beacon to guide ships into San Pedro Bay. It features gabled roofs, decorative cross beams, and hand-carved porch railings. The structure was blacked out during World War II and served as a U.S. Navy lookout tower before being restored and placed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1974.
san-pedro.Saint Peter's Episcopal Church: A historic Episcopal church serving the San Pedro waterfront community, reflecting the deep religious and cultural life of this working-class port neighborhood.
san-pedro.San Pedro Municipal Building: 1920s civic structure famous for the 'twist' walk in The Usual Suspects.
san-pedro.Sunken City: This site contains the ruins of a coastal neighborhood that began sliding into the ocean during a devastating 1929 landslide. Today, it is an unstable terrain of gnarled concrete foundations, ruined sidewalks, and vibrant graffiti art. Although it is technically a prohibited "No Trespassing" zone, it is a well-known local destination famous for its post-apocalyptic atmosphere and appearance in the film The Big Lebowski.
san-pedro.The Ghost Tracks: Remnants of the Pacific Electric Railway tracks that once ran along the bluff, now twisted and ending abruptly at the cliff edge.
san-pedro.Vincent Thomas Bridge: A 1,500-foot long suspension bridge that serves as the iconic gateway to the Port of Los Angeles.
san-pedro.Vinegar Hill HPOZ: A protected district featuring a collection of late 19th-century Queen Anne and Craftsman homes.
san-pedro.Warehouse One (Signal Street): A 1917 bonded warehouse; a massive concrete fortress frequently used as a backdrop for high-stakes urban drama.
san-pedro.Warner Grand Theatre: An opulent 1931 Art Deco movie palace designed by B. Marcus Priteca.

santa-clarita.St. Francis Dam Disaster Site: Hidden deep in a quiet, rugged canyon, these massive, scattered chunks of concrete are the haunting ruins of William Mulholland's 1928 dam collapse—the second-deadliest disaster in California history after the 1906 San Francisco earthquake.
santa-clarita.Swede's Cut: Swede's Cut, also known as the Big Cut, Castaic Cut, or Culebra Excavation, is the most significant man-made landmark on the original 1915 Ridge Route in the mountains north of Castaic. Completed in mid-1915, this massive excavation was a defining feat of early 20th-century California road building. The cut was dug 110 feet deep into the ridgeline. While much of the 1915 highway was constructed using manual labor and mule-drawn scrapers, the sheer scale of Swede's Cut required the use of steam shovels and dynamite. Construction of the cut involved moving approximately one million cubic yards of earth and rock.

santa-monica.Aero Theatre: Built in 1939 by P.M. Woolpert for aviation pioneer Donald Douglas, it originally served as a 24-hour cinema for Douglas Aircraft employees. It remains one of the last major repertory theaters in Los Angeles.
santa-monica.Big Dean's Ocean Front Cafe: Big Dean's Ocean Front Cafe. A landmark on the boardwalk since the 1970s (previously Loring's Lunchroom in 1902), it has served famous patrons like Arnold Schwarzenegger and Cary Grant. While the cafe itself is a staple, it is located directly at the foot of the Santa Monica Pier, a setting for countless films including The Sting, Forrest Gump, and The Net.
santa-monica.Camera Obscura Art Lab: Camera Obscura Art Lab. This site houses a rare, historically accurate public access camera obscura. Originally opened in 1898, it is the only remaining device of its kind in the United States.
santa-monica.Former Norma Talmadge French Normandy Es: Located on the historic "Gold Coast," this 1929 French Normandy estate was originally built for silent film star Norma Talmadge. It has been home to legendary figures including Cary Grant, Howard Hughes, and Princess Grace of Monaco.
santa-monica.Gehry Residence: Frank Gehry's iconic, deconstructivist personal home that wrapped an existing Dutch Colonial in corrugated metal, plywood, and chainlink.
santa-monica.Santa Monica Pier: An iconic 1909 landmark featuring the Pacific Park amusement park, a historic carousel, and the official end point of Route 66.
santa-monica.The John Entenza House:
Architect: Harwell Hamilton Harris

Architectural Style: Early California Modernism

The History: This is a pivotal site in architectural history. It was the residence of John Entenza, the editor of Arts & Architecture magazine, who collaborated here with Charles and Ray Eames to create the world-famous Case Study House Program in 1945.

Key Architectural Features: The home is known for its "scenographic" design, intended to feel like a film set. It features a unique carport with an outdoor shower and a seamless integration between the interior and the lush canyon landscape.

The stairs provide a direct hillside route between Sunset Boulevard and the high ridges of the neighborhood, offering a lung-burning climb rewarded at the summit by views of the Silver Lake Reservoir and, on clear days, the San Gabriel Mountains beyond. Part of the original network of pedestrian infrastructure built to connect early 20th-century residents to the streetcar lines below.

silver-lake.Neutra VDL Studio and Residences: The home and studio of pioneering Austrian-American architect Richard Neutra, and a National Historic Landmark. Built on the edge of the Silver Lake Reservoir, the VDL Research House embodies Neutra's philosophy of "biorealism" — the idea that architecture should blur the boundary between interior and exterior through massive windows, rooftop gardens, and a deep responsiveness to light, water, and landscape. The building is widely credited as the crucible of the "Silver Lake style" of light-filled, hillside modernist living that has defined the neighborhood's architectural identity ever since. Now managed by Cal Poly Pomona, it is open for public tours.

silver-lake.The Frog Pond / Lyric Hyperion: Currently operating as the Lyric Hyperion Theatre & Cafe, this building holds a vital place in Los Angeles LGBTQ+ history. During the 1970s and 80s, the space operated as 'The Frog Pond', a highly popular gay restaurant and cabaret. In 1980, the establishment was firebombed by an anti-gay assailant. The tragic blast injured three people and served as a major catalyst for the community; in direct response, the Sunset Junction Neighborhood Alliance was formed to ease neighborhood tensions and promote unity, eventually launching the famous Sunset Junction Street Fair.

south-gate.2440 Firestone Blvd. South Gate, CA 9028: Soshin Trade Corporation‎ - salvaged Japanese parts.
south-gate.Historic Firestone Tire and Rubber Compa: This is the site of the historic Firestone Tire and Rubber Company plant. Designed by the renowned architectural firm Curlett and Beelman (architects of the Eastern Columbia Building) and completed in 1928, it was Firestone’s first manufacturing facility outside of Akron, Ohio. The Italianate-style complex was so central to the city's identity that the main thoroughfare was renamed Firestone Boulevard in honor of Harvey Firestone. While much of the plant has been redeveloped, "Building 2" remains a significant historic survivor of the region's "Rubber Capital" era.
south-gate.Industrial Warehouse Corridor (Atlantic: A large-scale industrial warehouse typical of the Atlantic Avenue corridor. This area remains the backbone of the city's economy, supporting the logistics and manufacturing sectors that define the "Gateway" region.
south-gate.KIPP Coraz\u00f3n Academy: Home to KIPP Corazón Academy, a modern educational anchor. The site represents the city's transition toward contemporary community-focused land use on the historic Long Beach Boulevard corridor.
south-gate.Lido Night Club & Restaurant: Lido Night Club & Restaurant, a legendary South Gate institution. It is a premier destination for Mexican musical traditions, specifically Cumbia and Banda. Known for its vibrant atmosphere and live performances, it has been a cornerstone of local nightlife and culture for decades.
south-gate.Soshin Trade Corporation (STC): Home to Soshin Trade Corporation (STC). Established in 1988, this facility became a major regional hub for the distribution of Japanese automotive parts, reflecting the city's continued ties to the global automotive industry.
south-gate.South Gate Civic Center: A prominent complex featuring New Formalist and WPA-era design, representing the municipal growth of the city during the late 1930s.
south-gate.South Gate High School: The home of South Gate High School. The cornerstone of the original campus was laid in 1930, marking the beginning of the city's independent educational system. It remains a central community landmark and a prime example of the growth of the Gateway Cities during the early 20th century.
south-gate.South Gate Middle School: The site of South Gate Middle School (originally South Gate Junior High), constructed in 1941. This facility was built to accommodate the massive population boom the city experienced as it became an industrial powerhouse for the war effort.
south-gate.Southland Motel: The Southland Motel, a classic example of the mid-century roadside motels that lined Long Beach Boulevard. This thoroughfare was a primary route for travelers heading from Downtown Los Angeles to the harbor, resulting in a unique strip of 1940s and 50s hospitality architecture.
south-gate.The Firestone Plant Site (Ghost Site): The former site of one of the world's largest tire manufacturing plants; now repurposed, it remains a landmark of the city's blue-collar legacy.
south-gate.The Tweedy Mile: A classic mid-century 'Main Street' that served as the retail hub for the region's manufacturing workers; known for its Streamline Moderne storefronts and neon signage.

hcm-region-south-la-west-adams.Britt House (HCM #96): A 1910 Craftsman mansion that reflects the peak of residential design in the West Adams district.
hcm-region-south-la-west-adams.Dunbar Hotel (HCM #85): The cultural heart of Central Avenue jazz in the 1930s and 40s. A pivotal site in L.A.s Black history.
hcm-region-south-la-west-adams.St. Vincent de Paul Church (HCM #90): A Spanish Colonial Revival masterpiece built in 1925, noted for its ornate churrigueresque tile work and dome.

south-park._neighborhood: A historic neighborhood (separate from the DTLA South Park).
south-park.52nd Place Historic District: Originally called the 52nd Place - Tifal Brothers Tract, The entire streetscape between Figueroa and Flower Streets is a designated Historic Preservation Overlay Zone, functioning as an open-air museum of intact 1910s Craftsman bungalows. Walking the block offers a rare, uninterrupted glimpse into working-class Los Angeles before the booming postwar expansion.

south-pasadena.1001 Arroyo Verde Rd South Pasadena, Cal: Arroyo Seco Stables, a historic equestrian site.
south-pasadena.1055 Lohman Lane, South Pasadena, CA: Creekside golf course with a lighted driving range & a bar/restaurant hosting live jazz & blues.
south-pasadena.Biff Tannen's House (Back to the Future: This residence served as Biff Tannen’s house in Back to the Future Part II. It also gained later fame as the "Mitch-a-palooza" frat house in the 2003 comedy Old School.
south-pasadena.Classic 1925 Minimal Traditional Residen: A classic 1925 residence representative of the city's high-quality "Minimal Traditional" and Craftsman-influenced housing stock.
south-pasadena.Fair Oaks Pharmacy & Soda Fountain: Fair Oaks Pharmacy & Soda Fountain, an authentic 1915 drugstore that appeared in Mr. Deeds and Back to the Future.
south-pasadena.George McFly's House (Back to the Future: Famous as George McFly’s house in the 1985 classic Back to the Future. The street itself is a pilgrimage site for fans, as several other houses from the film are located on this same block.
south-pasadena.Hi-Life Burgers: Home to Hi-Life Burgers, a local institution that has been a community staple since 1978. It is renowned for its classic diner-style menu and is frequently cited by residents as having the best breakfast burritos in town.
south-pasadena.Historic Filming Residence (Cold Case): A frequent filming site that has appeared in various television productions, most notably in the series Cold Case.
south-pasadena.Historic Filming Street (Milan Ave): Known to location scouts as the "perfect street," this stretch is a hidden museum of Craftsman and Colonial Revival homes. It has stood in for "Haddonfield, Illinois" and "Anytown, USA" in dozens of films, yet remains a quiet, private neighborhood gem.
south-pasadena.Historic Residential Property (Mission W: A residential property located near the city’s historic Mission West District, an area characterized by its early 20th-century commercial and transit-oriented development.
south-pasadena.Laurie Strode's House (Halloween): Located just a short walk from the Myers house, this is the iconic home where Jamie Lee Curtis's character Laurie Strode sits on the brick retaining wall waiting for a ride in John Carpenter's Halloween (1978). The owners frequently keep fake foam pumpkins out for visiting fans.
south-pasadena.Pee-wee Herman's House (Pee-wee's Big Ad: Recognizable to fans as the exterior of Pee-wee Herman’s house in the 1985 film Pee-wee's Big Adventure. The house features the quintessential "small-town" look that director Tim Burton sought for the character.
south-pasadena.Pee-Wee's House: The colorful, quirky neighborhood home of Pee-Wee Herman in Tim Burton's Pee-Wee's Big Adventure.
south-pasadena.South Pasadena War Memorial Building: The South Pasadena War Memorial Building. Constructed in 1923, this rustic, shingle-style landmark serves as a tribute to WWI veterans. It features a tree planted by General Pershing and historic cannons on the grounds.
south-pasadena.The Garfield House (Greene & Greene): Known as the Garfield House, this is a masterpiece of the American Arts and Crafts movement designed by the legendary architects Greene & Greene (1903–1904). It was built for Lucretia Rudolph Garfield, the widow of President James A. Garfield.
south-pasadena.The Halloween (1978) House: The original house of Michael Myers from John Carpenter's classic horror film. (Relocated from its original spot).
south-pasadena.The Harper Family Home (Mama's Family): The primary filming location for the Harper family home in the long-running television series Mama's Family. It also made a brief appearance in the original Halloween.
south-pasadena.The Hedge Scene Location (Halloween, 197: Horror fans recognize this site for the "Hedge Scene" in the original 1978 Halloween, where Michael Myers famously disappears behind a hedge while being watched by Laurie Strode.
south-pasadena.The Iron Works: Now a restaurant, this 1880s building was originally a general store and hotel. It is one of the oldest commercial buildings in the city and features a rare, surviving example of 19th-century industrial brickwork.
south-pasadena.The Lambert/Foster Family Home (Step by: Served as the Lambert/Foster family home in the 1990s sitcom Step by Step.
south-pasadena.The Michael Myers House & The Century Ho: The Michael Myers House (The Century House), one of the city's oldest buildings and the primary filming location for the 1978 horror classic Halloween.
south-pasadena.The Michael Myers House (Halloween): Now deemed a South Pasadena Cultural Landmark, this two-story house is the infamous Haddonfield, Illinois, residence where a young Michael Myers murdered his sister in John Carpenter's Halloween (1978). It was moved a few blocks from its original filming location and restored.
south-pasadena.The Red Shoes: Local business on El Centro St.
south-pasadena.The Rialto Theatre: The Rialto Theatre, a 1925 Baroque-style movie palace that appeared in The Player and the premiere of "Stab" in Scream 2.

gateway-cities-southeast.Corona Avenue Elementary (Neutra): A rare educational commission by modernist Richard Neutra, bringing high-design to the Southeast corridor.
gateway-cities-southeast.Ed 'Big Daddy' Roth Studio: The birthplace of Rat Fink and Kustom Kulture; a site of massive subcultural significance.
gateway-cities-southeast.Pilgrim Fence Co. Elephant: A local vernacular landmark featuring a large elephant statue at an industrial site.

gateway-cities-southeast.Tiki Motel (Terminator Site): The actual filming location for Sarah Connor and Kyle Reese's hideout in the original Terminator.

studio-city.11837 Laurelwood Dr, Studio City, CA 916:
Architect: R.M. Schindler

The History: Designated as Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument No. 228, this 20-unit complex is the last and largest multi-family design created by master modernist architect R.M. Schindler.

Architectural Significance: Stepped elegantly into the hillside, the property is widely considered one of the finest Modern expressions of the courtyard apartment complex in Southern California. Schindler designed the layout so that each apartment feels like a standalone home, featuring unique sightlines, private outdoor spaces, and his signature exquisite wood detailing.
studio-city.12016 Moorpark St, North Hollywood, Los: 12016 Moorpark St, North Hollywood, Los Angeles, California 91607 - In "The 40 Year Old Virgin," Steve Carell lived in an apartment here 3.
studio-city.12036 Ventura Blvd Los Angeles, Californ: "It's been called a "culinary wax museum," the last of a dying breed, the kind of coffee shop Donna Reed took the family to for blue-plate specials.".
studio-city.12109 Ventura Place Studio City, CA 9160: Kit Kraft - started in 1946 - The secret spot in LA for wacky, unique, old, and curious small art supplies.
studio-city.4024 Radford Ave, Studio City, CA 91604: Vast production facility with 18 soundstages & backlot sets including a New York City street.
studio-city.4343 Babcock Ave: Identified by SurveyLA as an excellent individual example of American Colonial Revival residential architecture. Built in 1937, it represents the high-quality suburban design that attracted film industry professionals to the area during the Golden Age of Hollywood.
studio-city.Art's Delicatessen: A 1957 institution essential to the neighborhood's Jewish deli heritage and entertainment industry history.
studio-city.Book Star (Studio Theatre): A bookstore housed within a beautifully preserved 1930s movie palace.
studio-city.Carpenter Community Charter School: The social and geographic center of the Silver Triangle's high-density walking culture.
studio-city.Chemosphere: Built in 1960 and designed by John Lautner for aerospace engineer Leonard Malin, this futuristic Googie/Space Age Modernism home was famously described as 'the most modern home built in the world'. To solve the problem of an 'unbuildable' 45-degree sloped site, Lautner designed an octagonal, flying saucer-like house perched atop a single 29-foot-high, 5-foot-wide concrete column. The residence is accessed via a funicular (cable car) from the street level and features wrap-around windows offering uninterrupted, panoramic views of the San Fernando Valley. Despite its highly precarious look, the engineering is so incredibly sound that it has effortlessly survived major earthquakes.
studio-city.First Christian Church of North Hollywoo: Adjacent to the First Christian Church of North Hollywood, a 1949 Late American Colonial Revival structure recognized for its architectural integrity and community significance.
studio-city.Flying A Estate (Gene Autry House): The 1948 Roland Coate-designed ranch that anchored Fryman Canyon's 'Golden Age' of celebrity seclusion.
studio-city.Former Site of Hanna-Barbera Studios: The legendary former site of Hanna-Barbera Studios. Built in 1963 and designed by architect Arthur Froehlich, this futuristic "Jetsons-style" building served as the birthplace of The Flintstones, Scooby-Doo, and The Smurfs. Though now converted into the "Cahuenga Place Apartments," the site is recognized as one of the most culturally significant locations in the history of television animation.
studio-city.Historic 1937 Ventura Place Commercial B: A historic 1937 commercial building featuring exposed bow trusses and high ceilings. Located on the vibrant Ventura Place corridor near CBS Studios, it serves as a flagship retail anchor in one of the most high-traffic pedestrian zones in the Valley. This commercial hub is located immediately around the corner from the CBS Studios Headquarters, making it a frequent backdrop for local production activity.
studio-city.Laurelwood Apartments: Designated as Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument #228, this 20-unit hillside complex was master architect Rudolph M. Schindler's largest and final multi-family design. Built in 1948–1949, it is widely considered one of the finest modern courtyard apartment complexes in Southern California. Schindler brilliantly staggered the units along the hillside so that each apartment feels like a private residence.
studio-city.Malcolm in the Middle House: The real-world exterior of the messy, chaotic family home from Malcolm in the Middle. (The original home was extensively remodeled/rebuilt in 2011).
studio-city.Olive & Grill: The location of Olive & Grill, a popular Mediterranean destination. The site is characteristic of the upscale dining scene that defines the "Silver Triangle" area of Studio City.
studio-city.Oy Bar: Taking over the space of the old Oyster House, Oy Bar maintains the dark, moody "neighborhood tavern" feel of the original while serving highly acclaimed, modern bar food. It feels like a hidden local secret, bridging the gap between a classic dive and a high-end gastropub.
studio-city.Red Window Coffee: A charming, walk-up window located on a bustling corner of Ventura Boulevard. It is an iconic Studio City "micro-spot" that serves as a morning social hub for the neighborhood, known for its Australian-style coffee and minimalist, functional design.
studio-city.Saint Saviour's Chapel: A charming English Gothic chapel on the grounds of Camp Hollywoodland, representing the pastoral, retreat-oriented development of the Studio City hills in the early 20th century.
studio-city.Studio City Walk of Fame: A central point of the Studio City Walk of Fame, which consists of over 300 granite diamonds embedded in the sidewalk. Established in 2003, it honors the neighborhood's deep contributions to the entertainment industry, from the early days of Republic Pictures to the modern era of television.
studio-city.Studio Sushi: Located literally across the street from the Radford Studio gates, this is a quintessential "industry" spot. It is a straightforward, reliable neighborhood sushi bar that has served generations of writers, actors, and crew members during lunch breaks and after-wrap dinners.
studio-city.The 'Anywhere USA' Walking Streets: The heart of Colfax Meadows; characterized by a lack of sidewalks and vintage lamps that make it a favorite cinematic backdrop.
studio-city.The 'Colfax' Estate Row: A stretch of massive 20,000+ sq ft lots featuring a transition from mid-century sprawling ranches to contemporary Modern Farmhouse architecture.
studio-city.The Colfax Ave Footbridge: The Colfax Ave Footbridge, an iconic neighborhood site used for dramatic scenes in Magnolia and Boogie Nights.
studio-city.The Grossman House (El Paradiso): Grossman House. Also known as "El Paradiso," this "house of glass" was designed by renowned architect Raphael Soriano. It is a City of Los Angeles Cultural-Historic Monument (#638).
studio-city.The Little Brown Church in the Valley: The Little Brown Church in the Valley, a charming 1939 redwood structure. It is internationally famous as the site where Ronald and Nancy Reagan were married on March 4, 1952. The church has remained an iconic "open door" sanctuary and a frequent filming location for Hollywood productions.
studio-city.The Rick Dalton Residence: The Rick Dalton Residence, used as Leonardo DiCaprio's house in Quentin Tarantino’s Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.
studio-city.Wilacre Park & Dearing Trail: The public gateway to Fryman Canyon's ridges, offering views of the valley and proximity to exclusive estates.

sun-valley.9753 La Tuna Canyon Road Sun Valley, CA: Former residence of actor David Carradine.
sun-valley.Sons of Anarchy Clubhouse: The exterior filming location and backlot set for the SAMCRO clubhouse in the FX hit series Sons of Anarchy.
sun-valley.Sun Valley Branch Library: A central neighborhood library offering bilingual resources and community programming for the Sun Valley area.
sun-valley.The Pink Motel & Cadillac Jack's Cafe:
The History: Built in 1946 by Maximillian and Gladys Thomulka, this complex is a perfectly preserved slice of post-war roadside Americana. Sitting right on the historic San Fernando Road corridor, its striking pink stucco and classic neon signage made it an instant visual landmark.

Modern Era: The motel and cafe no longer operate as traditional businesses for the public. Instead, they have transitioned entirely into a highly sought-after, private filming location, frequently utilized for fashion shoots, music videos, and television shows requiring a flawless mid-century aesthetic. The property also features a fish-shaped swimming pool that is legendary in the skateboarding community.

sunland.9240 Foothill Boulevard San Fernando Val: Retail chain offering home goods, clothing, electronics & more, plus exclusive designer collections.
sunland.9955 Sunland Boulevard: Old-fashioned eatery serving classic Italian meals in a unique 1930s building with a spacious patio.
sunland.Apperson Street School: A long-standing Sunland-Tujunga elementary school providing education and a central community gathering point in the foothills.
sunland.Historic 1946 Stone and Wood Estate: A historic 1946 Stone and Wood Estate perched on over six acres of rugged canyon land. This "Architectural Gem" features over 20-foot vaulted ceilings with wood beams and represents the early mid-century retreat style that defined this part of the San Fernando Valley before suburbanization.
sunland.Historic Foothill Boulevard Commercial S: A commercial site located along the historic Foothill Boulevardcorridor, which served as the primary thoroughfare for travelers and settlers moving between the San Fernando and San Gabriel Valleys in the early 20th century.
sunland.Shadow Hills Equestrian Estate: A premier example of a Shadow Hills Equestrian Estate. This custom-built ranch-style home is situated in a "K-Zone" (equestrian-zoned) neighborhood, featuring a wrap-around veranda with panoramic canyon views and professional horse facilities, including a three-stall barn and a large arena.
sunland.Traditional Shadow Hills Residence: A traditional residential property located in the exclusive Shadow Hills enclave, a community famous for its lack of sidewalks and streetlights to preserve its rural, "Old West" atmosphere.

los-angeles-sunland-tujunga.Big Tujunga Wash Historic Bridge: An iconic bridge that appears in key scenes of the classic film Chinatown, representing the rural infrastructure of the era.
los-angeles-sunland-tujunga.Bolton Hall: Built in 1913, this river rock building serves as a historical museum; originally the community center for a utopian colony called the Little Landers.
los-angeles-sunland-tujunga.Old Vienna Gardens (Villa Cinzano): An unusual Hansel and Gretel style house originally an Austrian-themed restaurant before becoming Villa Cinzano.

sunset-strip.Chateau Marmont: A 1929 Gothic Revival 'castle' hotel and a primary site of Hollywood's private and cinematic lore.
sunset-strip.Rainbow Bar & Grill: A historic rock 'n' roll sanctuary; home to Lemmy Kilmister and the 'Hollywood Vampires' drinking club.
sunset-strip.The Comedy Store (Former Ciro's): A repurposed 1940s nightclub that became the epicenter of the American stand-up comedy boom.
sunset-strip.Whisky a Go Go: The definitive rock venue; birthplace of the go-go dancer and launchpad for L.A.'s most iconic bands.

los-angeles-sylmar.12347 San Fernando Rd, San Fernando, CA: Ron-dee Coffee Shop by Armet & Davis 3  - youarehere photographs.
los-angeles-sylmar.14831 Little Tujunga Canyon Rd: A "luxe re-imagining" of a nearly century-old hunting lodge situated on a massive multi-parcel estate. Located along the scenic Little Tujunga Canyon Road—a noted Southern California "scenic drive"—this 11-acre property is adjacent to the 700,000-acre Angeles National Forest and represents the area's deep history as a rugged mountain retreat.
los-angeles-sylmar.Buffalo Bruce's Mercantile: Buffalo Bruce’s Mercantile, a local culinary landmark known for its rustic, Western-themed atmosphere and specialized barbecue, serving as a popular neighborhood gathering spot.
los-angeles-sylmar.Discover Charter Prep: A focused high school campus in the northern Valley dedicated to college preparation and specialized learning.
los-angeles-sylmar.Griffith Ranch Historical Marker: Griffith Ranch, the historic site of D.W. Griffith’s early 20th-century film ranch. A historical plaque at this location commemorates its role as a primary shooting locale for early silent films and Westerns that helped establish the Southern California film industry.
los-angeles-sylmar.Hansen Dam & Recreation Area: Hansen Dam & Recreation Area, a massive 1940 flood control project that was the largest earthen dam of its type in the world upon completion. It features a notable 1.5-acre swim lake and has served as a filming location for numerous productions, most notably for the spillway scenes in Terminator 2: Judgment Day.
los-angeles-sylmar.Historic 1965 Retail Strip Center: A 1965 retail strip center located on a high-traffic corridor. It represents the post-war commercial development of the area and sits just blocks from the Sylmar/San Fernando Metrolink station, a vital transit link between the Valley and Downtown LA.
los-angeles-sylmar.Mid-Century Residence (Near Olive View): A residential property located near the Olive View-UCLA Medical Center, a major regional healthcare landmark that was famously rebuilt in its current Brutalist-inspired style following the 1971 San Fernando Earthquake.
los-angeles-sylmar.Olive View-UCLA Medical Center: Olive View-UCLA Medical Center, a significant architectural and historical landmark noted for its 1971 earthquake survival and its subsequent reconstruction in a distinct modern, Brutalist-influenced style.
los-angeles-sylmar.Olympic Coffee Shop (Former Jimbill's): Home to the Olympic Coffee Shop, a long-standing neighborhood institution that has operated under this name since 1984. Historically, this 1957 building was home to Jimbill's Coffee Shop (1964–1969), and it is recognized by the Valley Relics Museum as a significant survivor of mid-century roadside cafe culture in the Valley.
los-angeles-sylmar.Osceola Street Elementary School: A primary Sylmar schoolhouse that anchors its local residential neighborhood through educational and civic participation.
los-angeles-sylmar.San Fernando Pioneer Memorial Cemetery: San Fernando Pioneer Memorial Cemetery, established in 1874, it is the oldest non-sectarian cemetery in the San Fernando Valley and the final resting place of many of the area's earliest pioneers and Civil War veterans.
los-angeles-sylmar.Sylmar Studios: This site also houses Sylmar Studios, a massive 300,000-square-foot independent production facility. Opened recently, it features state-of-the-art sound stages and production offices, continuing the San Fernando Valley's legacy as a global entertainment hub.
los-angeles-sylmar.The Cascades (Los Angeles Aqueduct Termi: The Cascades, the terminus of the Los Angeles Aqueduct and a California Historical Landmark. This is the site where William Mulholland famously inaugurated the water system in 1913 with the words, "There it is. Take it." It also served as a pivotal filming location for the 1974 masterpiece Chinatown.
los-angeles-sylmar.The Fast & Furious Drag Strip: The industrial backdrop used for the defining street-racing scenes of the original 2001 film, cementing Sylmar's gritty industrial roads in cinematic history.
los-angeles-sylmar.The Great Wall of Los Angeles: The Great Wall of Los Angeles is a half-mile long masterpiece in the San Fernando Valley that depicts the history of California through the eyes of marginalized communities. Designed by Judith Baca and executed by over 400 community artists and youth. Stretches along the concrete retaining wall of the Coldwater Canyon Avenue stretch of the Tujunga Wash. One of the longest murals in the world, currently undergoing a massive expansion to include more recent history.
los-angeles-sylmar.The Nethercutt Collection & Tower: A 10-story functional tower housing one of the world's most significant collections of vintage automobiles and mechanical musical instruments; featuring a 'Great Hall' inspired by grand European palaces.
los-angeles-sylmar.Wildlife Learning Center: Wildlife Learning Center, a prominent zoological sanctuary and educational facility. It is noteworthy for its rescue and rehabilitation programs and serves as a major cultural and family destination in the north Valley.

tarzana.Bothwell Ranch: The San Fernando Valley's last remaining commercial orange grove; a 13-acre relic of the pre-war 'Citrus Belt'.
tarzana.Corbin Bowl: A classic 1958 neighborhood bowling alley and social staple with iconic mid-century signage.
tarzana.Paladino's: Legendary West Valley hard rock hub famously known as the home of the tribute band scene.
tarzana.Tarzana Child Care Center: A dedicated early childhood education facility serving families in the Tarzana/Reseda area.

temple-city.Temple City Civic Center: A pristine example of 1960s municipal architecture, representing the city's peak era of suburban development.
temple-city.The Bridal Path: Expansive, landscaped medians originally designed for horse-and-carriage travel, now serving as a signature greenbelt for the city's main thoroughfares.

toluca-lake.Frank Gehry Wells Fargo Branch: A Wells Fargo branch designed by the world-renowned architect Frank Gehry (completed in 1982). It is a celebrated example of deconstructivist architecture, designed to look like a fragmented "drawing of a building" with a façade that appears to balance precariously.
toluca-lake.Papoo's Hot Dog Show (Site): Long-standing neighborhood institution famous for its historic signage and iconic 'thinker' statue. Now demolished.
toluca-lake.Paty's Coffee House: A 1960 landmark diner known as a gathering spot for classic Hollywood elite.
toluca-lake.Paty's Restaurant: Home to Paty’s Restaurant, a legendary neighborhood diner since 1960. It is a world-famous celebrity hangout (regularly visited by stars like Frank Sinatra and Bob Hope) and is known for its "Green Room" where major Hollywood deals have been signed. It also served as a filming location for The Office (the site of Jan and Dwight's secret meeting) and Desperate Housewives.
toluca-lake.The 40-Year-Old Virgin Apartment: The exterior filming location for Andy Stitzer's apartment in the 2005 Judd Apatow film.
toluca-lake.The Bob Hope Estate: The Bob Hope Estate, a massive 1930s compound that served as the home of the comedy legend for over 60 years.
toluca-lake.World Savings Building (Gehry): A significant 1982 deconstructivist design by Frank Gehry, now housing a bank branch.

unincorporated-topanga.Inn of the Seventh Ray: Inn of the Seventh Ray, a historic creek-side restaurant frequently cited as the "most romantic" in Los Angeles.
unincorporated-topanga.The Topanga Tower: Known as the Topanga Tower, this is a notable "adaptive reuse" landmark. Originally a Cold War-era relay tower, it was transformed into a stunning modern residence and is considered one of the canyon's hidden architectural gems.
unincorporated-topanga.Topanga Canyon Inn Bed and Breakfast: The site of the Topanga Canyon Inn Bed and Breakfast. The architecture is designed to evoke the Mediterranean and Spanish Colonial Revival styles of the 1920s, specifically referencing the work of historic architects like Wallace Neff.
unincorporated-topanga.Topanga Seed & Feed: A historic commercial hub in the canyon that serves as a living connection to Topanga rural, equestrian, and artistic roots.
unincorporated-topanga.Waveview Coastal Neighborhood Residence: A residential property in a neighborhood known for its spectacular "Waveview" vistas of the Pacific Ocean and the Santa Monica Mountains, characteristic of Topanga's rugged, mid-century retreat atmosphere.
